# MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
## Complete Product & Technical Specification

**Version:** 2.0.0
**Date:** 2026-01-05
**Status:** Definitive Specification for Rebuild
**Audience:** UX/Product Team, Development Team, Stakeholders

---

## Document Purpose

This document serves as the **Single Source of Truth** for rebuilding the MMS (Metro Mileage Management System) from scratch. It consolidates all requirements, architecture decisions, UX specifications, and lessons learned from the initial Claude Code implementation.

The original implementation encountered complexity issues that resulted in incomplete features and inconsistent behavior. This specification provides a complete, unambiguous reference for human teams to deliver a production-ready demo.

---

## Table of Contents

### Part I: Business Context & Strategy

#### 1. Executive Summary
- 1.1 Project Overview
- 1.2 Business Objective
- 1.3 Success Metrics
- 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

> **Covers:** High-level project goals, the competitive bid context, what success looks like, and the 12-week timeline with key milestones.

#### 2. Railway Domain Fundamentals
- 2.1 Essential Glossary
- 2.2 Business Entities & Relationships
- 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy
- 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)
- 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

> **Covers:** Railway-specific terminology (EN 15380), entity relationships, maintenance intervals (PM1/PM2/HM), and domain constraints that are critical for credibility.

#### 3. Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning
- 3.1 Competitor Landscape
- 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis
- 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities
- 3.4 MMS Differentiators

> **Covers:** Analysis of DELMIA Quintiq, Tracsis, Railnova, SignatureRail; identified gaps in the market; and how MMS positions itself.

#### 4. Demo Strategy & Presentation
- 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth
- 4.2 Wow Moments
- 4.3 Credibility Checklist
- 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid
- 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)
- 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans

> **Covers:** How to win the bid through impressive demo, key moments that create impact, presentation flow, and contingency plans.

---

### Part II: User Experience & Design

#### 5. User Personas
- 5.1 Paul - The Maintenance Planner
- 5.2 Diana - The Dispatcher
- 5.3 Gabriel - The Fleet Manager
- 5.4 Design Implications per Persona

> **Covers:** Detailed persona profiles including goals, frustrations, device preferences, and how each persona influences UI decisions.

#### 6. User Journeys
- 6.1 Journey 1: Allocating a Train to Service (Diana)
- 6.2 Journey 2: Identifying and Resolving Imbalance (Paul)
- 6.3 Journey 3: Planning Maintenance Schedule (Paul)
- 6.4 Journey 4: Executive Review (Gabriel)
- 6.5 Journey Requirements Matrix

> **Covers:** Step-by-step user flows with scenes, expected times, click counts, and requirements revealed by each journey.

#### 7. Information Architecture
- 7.1 Navigation Structure
- 7.2 Drill-Down Hierarchy
- 7.3 Screen Inventory
- 7.4 Component Relationships

> **Covers:** How screens connect, navigation patterns, progressive disclosure hierarchy, and mental model alignment.

#### 8. Visual Design System
- 8.1 Design Principles
- 8.2 Color Palette & Semantic Colors
- 8.3 Typography Scale
- 8.4 Spacing & Layout Grid
- 8.5 Component Library (Blueprint.js v6)
- 8.6 Dark Mode Specification
- 8.7 Railway-Specific Visual Patterns

> **Covers:** Complete design token system, Blueprint.js integration, dark mode requirements, and enterprise UI patterns.

#### 9. Screen Specifications
- 9.1 Dashboard (Home)
- 9.2 Map View
- 9.3 Allocation Screen
- 9.4 Balancing Analysis
- 9.5 Simulation / What-If
- 9.6 Maintenance Gantt
- 9.7 Fleet List
- 9.8 Train Details

> **Covers:** Wireframes, component placement, data requirements, interactions, and acceptance criteria for each screen.

---

### Part III: Functional Requirements

#### 10. Requirements Overview
- 10.1 Functional Requirements Matrix
- 10.2 Non-Functional Requirements
- 10.3 Priority Classification (P0/P1/P2)
- 10.4 Scope Boundaries

> **Covers:** Complete FR/NFR tables with priorities, what's in scope vs explicitly out of scope.

#### 11. Module: Dashboard & KPIs
- 11.1 KPI Cards Specification
- 11.2 Traffic-Light Indicators
- 11.3 Depot Capacity Visualization
- 11.4 Maintenance Alerts
- 11.5 Drill-Down Navigation

> **Covers:** Detailed specs for the dashboard module with acceptance criteria and edge cases.

#### 12. Module: Map & Fleet Visualization
- 12.1 Map Provider & Configuration
- 12.2 Train Markers & Status Colors
- 12.3 Depot Markers & Capacity Indicators
- 12.4 Train Selection & Popup Cards
- 12.5 Auto-Refresh Mechanism
- 12.6 Clustering Behavior

> **Covers:** Map implementation details, marker specifications, and real-time update requirements.

#### 13. Module: Allocation with Recommendations
- 13.1 Service List & Filtering
- 13.2 Recommendation Engine Integration
- 13.3 Justification Display
- 13.4 One-Click Allocation
- 13.5 Train Filtering Criteria

> **Covers:** The core allocation workflow, solver integration, and justification presentation.

#### 14. Module: Km Balancing & Simulation
- 14.1 Distribution Visualization (Histogram)
- 14.2 Train Ranking by Delta
- 14.3 Outlier Identification
- 14.4 Scenario Creation
- 14.5 Simulation Execution
- 14.6 Scenario Comparison

> **Covers:** Balancing analytics, what-if simulation system, and scenario management.

#### 15. Module: Maintenance Gantt
- 15.1 Timeline View Options
- 15.2 Maintenance Blocks (Color-Coded)
- 15.3 Drag-Drop Scheduling
- 15.4 Constraint Validation
- 15.5 Depot Capacity Heatmap

> **Covers:** Gantt chart implementation, drag-drop interactions, and conflict detection.

---

### Part IV: Technical Architecture

#### 16. System Architecture
- 16.1 High-Level Architecture Diagram
- 16.2 Technology Stack
- 16.3 Monorepo Structure
- 16.4 Service Communication
- 16.5 Key Architectural Decisions

> **Covers:** Overall system design, technology choices with rationale, and project structure.

#### 17. Frontend Architecture
- 17.1 Next.js 14 App Router Structure
- 17.2 Component Organization
- 17.3 State Management (@zazcart/toolkit)
- 17.4 API Client Pattern
- 17.5 Blueprint.js Integration
- 17.6 Styling Strategy

> **Covers:** Frontend technical details, component patterns, and state management approach.

#### 18. Backend Architecture
- 18.1 Fastify Server Structure
- 18.2 Route Organization
- 18.3 Service Layer Pattern
- 18.4 Database Access (Drizzle ORM)
- 18.5 Caching Strategy (Redis)
- 18.6 Error Handling

> **Covers:** Backend implementation patterns, API design, and data access layer.

#### 19. Database Design
- 19.1 Entity-Relationship Diagram
- 19.2 Schema Definition (All Tables)
- 19.3 Index Strategy
- 19.4 Data Types & Constraints
- 19.5 Seed Data Requirements

> **Covers:** Complete database schema, relationships, indexes, and realistic seed data specifications.

#### 20. Solver Architecture (Python/OR-Tools)
- 20.1 FastAPI Service Structure
- 20.2 CP-SAT Optimization Model
- 20.3 Objective Function
- 20.4 Constraints Definition
- 20.5 Justification Generation
- 20.6 Fallback Strategy

> **Covers:** Python solver implementation, OR-Tools model, and recommendation engine logic.

#### 21. API Specification
- 21.1 REST Endpoints Inventory
- 21.2 Request/Response Schemas
- 21.3 Error Response Format
- 21.4 Authentication (Demo Scope)

> **Covers:** Complete API documentation with all endpoints, schemas, and examples.

---

### Part V: Implementation Guide

#### 22. Epic & Story Breakdown
- 22.1 Epic Overview
- 22.2 Epic 1: Project Foundation
- 22.3 Epic 2: Dashboard & KPIs
- 22.4 Epic 3: Map & Fleet Visualization
- 22.5 Epic 4: Allocation with Recommendations
- 22.6 Epic 5: Km Balancing & Simulation
- 22.7 Epic 6: Maintenance Gantt
- 22.8 Story Dependencies

> **Covers:** Complete breakdown of work into implementable stories with acceptance criteria.

#### 23. Development Setup
- 23.1 Prerequisites
- 23.2 Environment Variables
- 23.3 Docker Compose Configuration
- 23.4 Database Setup & Seeding
- 23.5 Running the Development Environment

> **Covers:** Step-by-step setup instructions for new developers.

#### 24. Testing Strategy
- 24.1 Unit Testing (Vitest)
- 24.2 E2E Testing (Playwright)
- 24.3 Visual Testing Requirements
- 24.4 Test Coverage Goals

> **Covers:** Testing approach, tools, and mandatory verification requirements.

#### 25. Deployment & Demo Environment
- 25.1 Demo Laptop Setup
- 25.2 Docker Compose Production
- 25.3 Demo Reset Script
- 25.4 Fallback Screens

> **Covers:** How to deploy for demo, reset procedures, and contingency materials.

---

### Part VI: Lessons Learned & Recovery

#### 26. Original Implementation Issues
- 26.1 What Went Wrong
- 26.2 Broken Features Analysis
- 26.3 Technical Debt Accumulated
- 26.4 Root Cause Analysis

> **Covers:** Honest assessment of Claude Code implementation issues to avoid repeating mistakes.

#### 27. Recovery Recommendations
- 27.1 Recommended Rebuild Approach

# PART I: BUSINESS CONTEXT & STRATEGY

---

## Chapter 1: Executive Summary

### 1.1 Project Overview

**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.

**Core Problem Statement:**

Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:

1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints

**Solution Vision:**

MMS addresses all three pain points through:
- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
- What-if simulation for strategic planning

### 1.2 Business Objective

**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.

**Target Audience:**
- Operations Director (decision maker)
- Client's CTO (technical validator)
- Maintenance leadership (domain experts)

**Success Statement:**

> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**

**Key Differentiators from Competitors:**

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
| Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |

### 1.3 Success Metrics

**User Success Metrics:**

| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |

**Business Success Metrics:**

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Demo Duration | 15 minutes maximum |
| Demo Stability | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures |
| Domain Terminology | 100% correct EN 15380 railway terms |
| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" |

**Technical Success Metrics:**

| Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|-------------|--------|-----------|
| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in live demo |
| Map Update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Maintain interactive feel |
| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Critical for presentation |

### 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

**Project Timeline:** 12 weeks

**Milestone Schedule:**

| Week | Milestone | Deliverable |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Week 4 | Core solver working | Proof of technical capability |
| Week 6 | Navigable UI with mocked data | Visual credibility established |
| Week 8 | End-to-end flow working | Integration proof complete |
| Week 10 | Specialist validation passed | Domain credibility confirmed |
| Week 12 | Demo-ready with fallbacks | Risk mitigation complete |

**Technical Constraints:**

| Constraint | Impact | Mitigation |
|------------|--------|------------|
| 12-week timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus exclusively on P0 features |
| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Use seed data, skip authentication |
| Python solver requirement | Cross-language integration | JSON REST API between services |
| OR-Tools learning curve | Development complexity | Simplify model, use greedy fallback |

**Key Assumptions:**

1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support required
4. English UI only (no internationalization)
5. Desktop-primary, tablet-secondary (no mobile optimization)

---

## Chapter 2: Railway Domain Fundamentals

### 2.1 Essential Glossary

All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.

**Core Terms:**

| Term | Definition | NOT Acceptable |
|------|------------|----------------|
| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |

**Maintenance Terms:**

| Term | Definition | Typical Interval |
|------|------------|------------------|
| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |

**Operational Terms:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |

**Cardinality Rules:**

- One Fleet has many Trains (1:N)
- One Train belongs to one Fleet (N:1)
- One Depot can host trains from multiple Fleets (N:M)
- One Train has one Base Depot (N:1)
- One Service can have many Allocations (different dates) (1:N)
- One Train can have many Allocations (different services) (1:N)
- One Train can have many Maintenance Schedules (1:N)

### 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy

**Maintenance Cascade:**

```

     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Note: Includes PM1 tasks

### 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)

These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.

**Hard Constraints (Never Violate):**

1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization

**Soft Constraints (Optimize Within):**

1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)

**Critical Timing Rules:**

| Rule | Specification |
|------|---------------|
| PM1 tolerance window | Can be Вұ500 km from trigger |
| PM2 tolerance window | Can be Вұ1,000 km from trigger |
| HM tolerance window | Can be Вұ2,000 km from trigger |
| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |

### 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

Explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and set correct expectations:

**Phase 2+ (Not in Demo):**

| Feature | Reason for Exclusion | Target Phase |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| MILP long-term optimization | Exceeds complexity budget | Phase 2 |
| Real EAM system integration | Requires client environment | Phase 2 |
| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |

**Explicitly NOT Goals:**

- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
- This is NOT a timetabling system
- This is NOT a revenue management system
- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems

---

## Chapter 3: Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning

### 3.1 Competitor Landscape

**Primary Competitors:**

| Competitor | Focus Area | Strength | Weakness |
|------------|------------|----------|----------|
| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |

### 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis

SignatureRail represents the most direct competitor in terms of user experience and feature set.

**SignatureRail Strengths:**

- Clean, modern enterprise UI
- Well-organized workflow panels
- Good use of data tables
- Professional color scheme
- Clear navigation structure

**SignatureRail Weaknesses (MMS Opportunities):**

1. **Basic km tracking without optimization** - Shows variance but doesn't recommend actions
2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios
4. **Separate maintenance and operations** - Silos remain
5. **No map visualization** - Spatial context missing

### 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities

**Gap Analysis:**

| Gap | Market Need | MMS Solution |
|-----|-------------|--------------|
| Passive vs Active Balancing | Systems show variance but don't optimize | AI-powered recommendations with justifications |
| Black Box Decisions | Users don't understand why system recommends | Transparent 3+ reason justifications |
| Operations/Maintenance Silos | Separate systems, manual coordination | Unified interface for both departments |
| Strategic Planning | Day-to-day focus, no simulation | What-if scenarios for planning |
| Dead Running Visibility | Hidden cost, rarely tracked | Explicit display and optimization |

### 3.4 MMS Differentiators

**Primary Differentiators:**

1. **Km Balancing as Core Objective**
   - Not a secondary feature or afterthought
   - Drives all allocation recommendations
   - Visible in dashboard KPIs

2. **Transparent Justifications**
   - Every recommendation shows 3+ reasons
   - Human-readable explanations
   - Builds trust vs black box

3. **Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations**
   - Single interface for both departments
   - Recommendations consider maintenance schedule
   - Reduces coordination overhead

4. **Accessible Simulation**
   - Decision-makers can test scenarios
   - Side-by-side comparison
   - No technical expertise required

5. **Visible Dead Running**
   - Hidden cost made explicit
   - Factored into optimization
   - ROI clearly demonstrable

---

## Chapter 4: Demo Strategy & Presentation

### 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth

> **Mantra:** "Solving 1 pain point extremely well > solving 10 pain points poorly"

**Strategic Focus:**

The demo should demonstrate deep understanding of the km balancing problem rather than showcase many features superficially. Every feature shown must:

1. Be 100% functional (no "coming soon")
2. Use correct railway terminology
3. Show real (simulated but realistic) data
4. Complete end-to-end without errors

### 4.2 Wow Moments

These are specific moments in the demo designed to create impact:

**Wow Moment 1: The Variance Discovery**
- Dashboard loads showing 18% variance (yellow warning)
- Client recognizes their exact problem
- "They're showing us what we live every day"

**Wow Moment 2: The Justification Card**
- Recommendation shows not just WHAT but WHY
- 3+ human-readable reasons
- "Finally, a system that explains itself"

**Wow Moment 3: The What-If**
- Create scenario, adjust allocations
- See projected impact in < 10 seconds
- "I can test my ideas before committing"

**Wow Moment 4: The Map Context**
- See where all trains are spatially
- Click train, see km delta immediately
- "This gives us operational awareness"

### 4.3 Credibility Checklist

Before demo, verify:

- [ ] All terminology is EN 15380 compliant
- [ ] Depot names are real Portuguese locations
- [ ] Service codes follow realistic patterns (IC, R, U)
- [ ] Maintenance intervals are realistic (PM1: 15k km)
- [ ] Train IDs follow realistic naming (UTE-3400-xxx)
- [ ] Km values are in plausible ranges (30k-60k)
- [ ] Variance percentages are realistic (10-25%)
- [ ] Map shows Portugal correctly positioned

### 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid

**NEVER do these in demo:**

| Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Alternative |
|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Show stub screens | Destroys credibility | Show fewer, complete features |
| Use placeholder data | "Lorem ipsum" is unprofessional | Use realistic railway data |
| Demo features that don't work | Client will try to break it | Only show stable features |
| Use generic software terms | "Vehicle" instead of "Train" | Use railway terminology |
| Show loading spinners | Suggests slow system | Pre-cache or mock fast responses |
| Apologize for missing features | Shows lack of confidence | Present what exists as intentional |

### 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)

**Minutes 0-2: Context Setting**
- "We understand your km balancing challenge"
- Show dashboard with variance indicator
- "This is what you see every day"

**Minutes 2-5: The Problem Quantified**
- Click variance to see distribution
- Show outliers (5 over-running, 4 under-running)
- "These 9 trains are costing you money"

**Minutes 5-8: The Solution in Action**
- Navigate to Allocation
- Select a pending service
- Show recommendations with justifications
- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
- Confirm allocation with one click

**Minutes 8-11: Planning Ahead**
- Navigate to Simulation
- Create what-if scenario
- Adjust allocations for under-running trains
- Show projected variance improvement
- "Test your strategy before committing"

**Minutes 11-13: The Big Picture**
- Return to Dashboard
- Show map with train positions
- Show depot capacity
- "Everything in one view"

**Minutes 13-15: Q&A Buffer**
- Prepared questions if no questions asked
- Have fallback screens ready

### 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans

**Anticipated Questions:**

| Question | Prepared Answer |
|----------|-----------------|
| "How does the solver work?" | "CP-SAT optimization balancing multiple constraints - we can show the logic" |
| "Can it integrate with our EAM?" | "Yes, REST API design allows integration with any EAM system" |
| "What about real-time GPS?" | "Architecture supports real-time feeds, demo uses simulated positions" |
| "How long to implement?" | "Core system in 3 months, customization depends on requirements" |
| "What if solver times out?" | "Fallback to simpler ranking ensures always having recommendations" |

**Fallback Plans:**

| Failure | Fallback |
|---------|----------|
| Solver timeout | Pre-computed recommendations in database |
| Database connection lost | Static JSON fallback data |
| Map fails to load | Table view of train positions |
| Browser crash | Second laptop with same setup |
| Network issues | Everything runs locally |

   344вҶ’---
   345вҶ’
   346вҶ’## Chapter 1: Executive Summary
   347вҶ’
   348вҶ’### 1.1 Project Overview
   349вҶ’
   350вҶ’**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.
   351вҶ’
   352вҶ’**Core Problem Statement:**
   353вҶ’
   354вҶ’Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:
   355вҶ’
   356вҶ’1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
   357вҶ’2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
   358вҶ’3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints
   359вҶ’
   360вҶ’**Solution Vision:**
   361вҶ’
   362вҶ’MMS addresses all three pain points through:
   363вҶ’- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
   364вҶ’- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
   365вҶ’- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
   366вҶ’- What-if simulation for strategic planning
   367вҶ’
   368вҶ’### 1.2 Business Objective
   369вҶ’
   370вҶ’**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.
   371вҶ’
   372вҶ’**Target Audience:**
   373вҶ’- Operations Director (decision maker)
   374вҶ’- Client's CTO (technical validator)
   375вҶ’- Maintenance leadership (domain experts)
   376вҶ’
   377вҶ’**Success Statement:**
   378вҶ’
   379вҶ’> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**
   380вҶ’
   381вҶ’**Key Differentiators from Competitors:**
   382вҶ’
   383вҶ’| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
   384вҶ’|----------------|----------------|
   385вҶ’| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
   386вҶ’| Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
   387вҶ’| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
   388вҶ’| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
   389вҶ’| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |
   390вҶ’
   391вҶ’### 1.3 Success Metrics
   392вҶ’
   393вҶ’**User Success Metrics:**
   394вҶ’
   395вҶ’| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
   396вҶ’|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
   397вҶ’| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
   398вҶ’| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
   399вҶ’| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
   400вҶ’| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
   401вҶ’| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
   402вҶ’| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
   403вҶ’| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |
   404вҶ’
   405вҶ’**Business Success Metrics:**
   406вҶ’
   407вҶ’| Metric | Target |
   408вҶ’|--------|--------|
   409вҶ’| Demo Duration | 15 minutes maximum |
   410вҶ’| Demo Stability | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures |
   411вҶ’| Domain Terminology | 100% correct EN 15380 railway terms |
   412вҶ’| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" |
   413вҶ’
   414вҶ’**Technical Success Metrics:**
   415вҶ’
   416вҶ’| Requirement | Target | Rationale |
   417вҶ’|-------------|--------|-----------|
   418вҶ’| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
   419вҶ’| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in live demo |
   420вҶ’| Map Update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
   421вҶ’| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Maintain interactive feel |
   422вҶ’| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Critical for presentation |
   423вҶ’
   424вҶ’### 1.4 Timeline & Constraints
   425вҶ’
   426вҶ’**Project Timeline:** 12 weeks
   427вҶ’
   428вҶ’**Milestone Schedule:**
   429вҶ’
   430вҶ’| Week | Milestone | Deliverable |
   431вҶ’|------|-----------|-------------|
   432вҶ’| Week 4 | Core solver working | Proof of technical capability |
   433вҶ’| Week 6 | Navigable UI with mocked data | Visual credibility established |
   434вҶ’| Week 8 | End-to-end flow working | Integration proof complete |
   435вҶ’| Week 10 | Specialist validation passed | Domain credibility confirmed |
   436вҶ’| Week 12 | Demo-ready with fallbacks | Risk mitigation complete |
   437вҶ’
   438вҶ’**Technical Constraints:**
   439вҶ’
   440вҶ’| Constraint | Impact | Mitigation |
   441вҶ’|------------|--------|------------|
   442вҶ’| 12-week timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus exclusively on P0 features |
   443вҶ’| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Use seed data, skip authentication |
   444вҶ’| Python solver requirement | Cross-language integration | JSON REST API between services |
   445вҶ’| OR-Tools learning curve | Development complexity | Simplify model, use greedy fallback |
   446вҶ’
   447вҶ’**Key Assumptions:**
   448вҶ’
   449вҶ’1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
   450вҶ’2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
   451вҶ’3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support required
   452вҶ’4. English UI only (no internationalization)
   453вҶ’5. Desktop-primary, tablet-secondary (no mobile optimization)
   454вҶ’

   457вҶ’## Chapter 2: Railway Domain Fundamentals
   458вҶ’
   459вҶ’### 2.1 Essential Glossary
   460вҶ’
   461вҶ’All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.
   462вҶ’
   463вҶ’**Core Terms:**
   464вҶ’
   465вҶ’| Term | Definition | NOT Acceptable |
   466вҶ’|------|------------|----------------|
   467вҶ’| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
   468вҶ’| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
   469вҶ’| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
   470вҶ’| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
   471вҶ’| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
   472вҶ’| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
   473вҶ’| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
   474вҶ’| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |
   475вҶ’
   476вҶ’**Maintenance Terms:**
   477вҶ’
   478вҶ’| Term | Definition | Typical Interval |
   479вҶ’|------|------------|------------------|
   480вҶ’| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
   481вҶ’| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
   482вҶ’| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
   483вҶ’| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |
   484вҶ’
   485вҶ’**Operational Terms:**
   486вҶ’
   487вҶ’| Term | Definition |
   488вҶ’|------|------------|
   489вҶ’| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
   490вҶ’| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
   491вҶ’| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
   492вҶ’| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
   493вҶ’| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |
   494вҶ’

   532вҶ’**Cardinality Rules:**
   533вҶ’
   534вҶ’- One Fleet has many Trains (1:N)
   535вҶ’- One Train belongs to one Fleet (N:1)
   536вҶ’- One Depot can host trains from multiple Fleets (N:M)
   537вҶ’- One Train has one Base Depot (N:1)
   538вҶ’- One Service can have many Allocations (different dates) (1:N)
   539вҶ’- One Train can have many Allocations (different services) (1:N)
   540вҶ’- One Train can have many Maintenance Schedules (1:N)
   541вҶ’
   542вҶ’### 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy
   543вҶ’
   544вҶ’**Maintenance Cascade:**

   548вҶ’ в””в”Җ Maintenance Profile

   552вҶ’     в”Ӯ

   555вҶ’     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Tasks: Component replacement, deep inspection
   556вҶ’     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Note: Includes PM1 tasks
   557вҶ’     в”Ӯ
   558вҶ’     в”ңв”Җ HM (every 90,000 km)
   559вҶ’     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Duration: 7-14 days
   560вҶ’     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Tasks: Major component overhaul, bogie work
   561вҶ’     в”Ӯ   в””в”Җ Note: Includes PM1 + PM2 tasks
   562вҶ’     в”Ӯ
   563вҶ’     в””в”Җ OVH (every 6 years or 500,000 km)

   565вҶ’         в””в”Җ Tasks: Complete refurbishment
   566вҶ’         в””в”Җ Note: Resets maintenance counters
   567вҶ’```

   597вҶ’### 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)
   598вҶ’
   599вҶ’These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.
   600вҶ’
   601вҶ’**Hard Constraints (Never Violate):**
   602вҶ’
   603вҶ’1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
   604вҶ’2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
   605вҶ’3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
   606вҶ’4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
   607вҶ’5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization
   608вҶ’
   609вҶ’**Soft Constraints (Optimize Within):**
   610вҶ’
   611вҶ’1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
   612вҶ’2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
   613вҶ’3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
   614вҶ’4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)
   615вҶ’
   616вҶ’**Critical Timing Rules:**
   617вҶ’
   618вҶ’| Rule | Specification |
   619вҶ’|------|---------------|
   620вҶ’| PM1 tolerance window | Can be Вұ500 km from trigger |
   621вҶ’| PM2 tolerance window | Can be Вұ1,000 km from trigger |
   622вҶ’| HM tolerance window | Can be Вұ2,000 km from trigger |
   623вҶ’| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
   624вҶ’| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |
   625вҶ’
   626вҶ’### 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do
   627вҶ’
   628вҶ’Explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and set correct expectations:
   629вҶ’
   630вҶ’**Phase 2+ (Not in Demo):**
   631вҶ’
   632вҶ’| Feature | Reason for Exclusion | Target Phase |
   633вҶ’|---------|---------------------|--------------|
   634вҶ’| MILP long-term optimization | Exceeds complexity budget | Phase 2 |
   635вҶ’| Real EAM system integration | Requires client environment | Phase 2 |
   636вҶ’| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
   637вҶ’| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
   638вҶ’| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
   639вҶ’| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
   640вҶ’| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
   641вҶ’| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |
   642вҶ’
   643вҶ’**Explicitly NOT Goals:**
   644вҶ’
   645вҶ’- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
   646вҶ’- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
   647вҶ’- This is NOT a timetabling system
   648вҶ’- This is NOT a revenue management system
   649вҶ’- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
   650вҶ’- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems

   653вҶ’
   654вҶ’## Chapter 3: Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning
   655вҶ’
   656вҶ’### 3.1 Competitor Landscape
   657вҶ’
   658вҶ’**Primary Competitors:**
   659вҶ’
   660вҶ’| Competitor | Focus Area | Strength | Weakness |
   661вҶ’|------------|------------|----------|----------|
   662вҶ’| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
   663вҶ’| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
   664вҶ’| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
   665вҶ’| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |
   666вҶ’
   667вҶ’### 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis
   668вҶ’
   669вҶ’SignatureRail represents the most direct competitor in terms of user experience and feature set.
   670вҶ’
   671вҶ’**SignatureRail Strengths:**
   672вҶ’
   673вҶ’- Clean, modern enterprise UI
   674вҶ’- Well-organized workflow panels
   675вҶ’- Good use of data tables
   676вҶ’- Professional color scheme
   677вҶ’- Clear navigation structure
   678вҶ’
   679вҶ’**SignatureRail Weaknesses (MMS Opportunities):**
   680вҶ’
   681вҶ’1. **Basic km tracking without optimization** - Shows variance but doesn't recommend actions
   682вҶ’2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
   683вҶ’3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios
   684вҶ’4. **Separate maintenance and operations** - Silos remain
   685вҶ’5. **No map visualization** - Spatial context missing
   686вҶ’
   687вҶ’### 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities
   688вҶ’
   689вҶ’**Gap Analysis:**
   690вҶ’
   691вҶ’| Gap | Market Need | MMS Solution |
   692вҶ’|-----|-------------|--------------|
   693вҶ’| Passive vs Active Balancing | Systems show variance but don't optimize | AI-powered recommendations with justifications |
   694вҶ’| Black Box Decisions | Users don't understand why system recommends | Transparent 3+ reason justifications |
   695вҶ’| Operations/Maintenance Silos | Separate systems, manual coordination | Unified interface for both departments |
   696вҶ’| Strategic Planning | Day-to-day focus, no simulation | What-if scenarios for planning |
   697вҶ’| Dead Running Visibility | Hidden cost, rarely tracked | Explicit display and optimization |
   698вҶ’
   699вҶ’### 3.4 MMS Differentiators
   700вҶ’
   701вҶ’**Primary Differentiators:**
   702вҶ’
   703вҶ’1. **Km Balancing as Core Objective**
   704вҶ’   - Not a secondary feature or afterthought
   705вҶ’   - Drives all allocation recommendations
   706вҶ’   - Visible in dashboard KPIs
   707вҶ’
   708вҶ’2. **Transparent Justifications**
   709вҶ’   - Every recommendation shows 3+ reasons
   710вҶ’   - Human-readable explanations
   711вҶ’   - Builds trust vs black box
   712вҶ’
   713вҶ’3. **Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations**
   714вҶ’   - Single interface for both departments
   715вҶ’   - Recommendations consider maintenance schedule
   716вҶ’   - Reduces coordination overhead
   717вҶ’
   718вҶ’4. **Accessible Simulation**
   719вҶ’   - Decision-makers can test scenarios
   720вҶ’   - Side-by-side comparison
   721вҶ’   - No technical expertise required
   722вҶ’
   723вҶ’5. **Visible Dead Running**
   724вҶ’   - Hidden cost made explicit
   725вҶ’   - Factored into optimization
   726вҶ’   - ROI clearly demonstrable
   727вҶ’
   728вҶ’---
   729вҶ’
   730вҶ’## Chapter 4: Demo Strategy & Presentation
   731вҶ’
   732вҶ’### 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth
   733вҶ’
   734вҶ’> **Mantra:** "Solving 1 pain point extremely well > solving 10 pain points poorly"
   735вҶ’
   736вҶ’**Strategic Focus:**
   737вҶ’
   738вҶ’The demo should demonstrate deep understanding of the km balancing problem rather than showcase many features superficially. Every feature shown must:
   739вҶ’
   740вҶ’1. Be 100% functional (no "coming soon")
   741вҶ’2. Use correct railway terminology
   742вҶ’3. Show real (simulated but realistic) data
   743вҶ’4. Complete end-to-end without errors
   744вҶ’
   745вҶ’### 4.2 Wow Moments
   746вҶ’
   747вҶ’These are specific moments in the demo designed to create impact:
   748вҶ’
   749вҶ’**Wow Moment 1: The Variance Discovery**
   750вҶ’- Dashboard loads showing 18% variance (yellow warning)
   751вҶ’- Client recognizes their exact problem
   752вҶ’- "They're showing us what we live every day"
   753вҶ’
   754вҶ’**Wow Moment 2: The Justification Card**
   755вҶ’- Recommendation shows not just WHAT but WHY
   756вҶ’- 3+ human-readable reasons
   757вҶ’- "Finally, a system that explains itself"
   758вҶ’
   759вҶ’**Wow Moment 3: The What-If**
   760вҶ’- Create scenario, adjust allocations
   761вҶ’- See projected impact in < 10 seconds
   762вҶ’- "I can test my ideas before committing"
   763вҶ’
   764вҶ’**Wow Moment 4: The Map Context**
   765вҶ’- See where all trains are spatially
   766вҶ’- Click train, see km delta immediately
   767вҶ’- "This gives us operational awareness"
   768вҶ’
   769вҶ’### 4.3 Credibility Checklist
   770вҶ’
   771вҶ’Before demo, verify:
   772вҶ’
   773вҶ’- [ ] All terminology is EN 15380 compliant
   774вҶ’- [ ] Depot names are real Portuguese locations
   775вҶ’- [ ] Service codes follow realistic patterns (IC, R, U)
   776вҶ’- [ ] Maintenance intervals are realistic (PM1: 15k km)
   777вҶ’- [ ] Train IDs follow realistic naming (UTE-3400-xxx)
   778вҶ’- [ ] Km values are in plausible ranges (30k-60k)
   779вҶ’- [ ] Variance percentages are realistic (10-25%)
   780вҶ’- [ ] Map shows Portugal correctly positioned
   781вҶ’
   782вҶ’### 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid
   783вҶ’
   784вҶ’**NEVER do these in demo:**
   785вҶ’
   786вҶ’| Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Alternative |
   787вҶ’|--------------|--------------|-------------|
   788вҶ’| Show stub screens | Destroys credibility | Show fewer, complete features |
   789вҶ’| Use placeholder data | "Lorem ipsum" is unprofessional | Use realistic railway data |
   790вҶ’| Demo features that don't work | Client will try to break it | Only show stable features |
   791вҶ’| Use generic software terms | "Vehicle" instead of "Train" | Use railway terminology |
   792вҶ’| Show loading spinners | Suggests slow system | Pre-cache or mock fast responses |
   793вҶ’| Apologize for missing features | Shows lack of confidence | Present what exists as intentional |
   794вҶ’
   795вҶ’### 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)
   796вҶ’

   807вҶ’**Minutes 5-8: The Solution in Action**

   810вҶ’- Show recommendations with justifications
   811вҶ’- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
   812вҶ’- Confirm allocation with one click
   813вҶ’
   814вҶ’**Minutes 8-11: Planning Ahead**

   821вҶ’**Minutes 11-13: The Big Picture**

   831вҶ’### 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans
   832вҶ’
   833вҶ’**Anticipated Questions:**

   843вҶ’**Fallback Plans:**

# PART I: BUSINESS CONTEXT & STRATEGY

---

## Chapter 1: Executive Summary

### 1.1 Project Overview

**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.

**Core Problem Statement:**

Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:

1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints

**Solution Vision:**

MMS addresses all three pain points through:
- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
- What-if simulation for strategic planning

### 1.2 Business Objective

**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.

**Target Audience:**
- Operations Director (decision maker)
- Client's CTO (technical validator)
- Maintenance leadership (domain experts)

**Success Statement:**

> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**

**Key Differentiators from Competitors:**

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
| Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |

### 1.3 Success Metrics

**User Success Metrics:**

| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |

**Business Success Metrics:**

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Demo Duration | 15 minutes maximum |
| Demo Stability | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures |
| Domain Terminology | 100% correct EN 15380 railway terms |
| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" |

**Technical Success Metrics:**

| Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|-------------|--------|-----------|
| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in live demo |
| Map Update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Maintain interactive feel |
| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Critical for presentation |

### 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

**Project Timeline:** 12 weeks

**Milestone Schedule:**

| Week | Milestone | Deliverable |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Week 4 | Core solver working | Proof of technical capability |
| Week 6 | Navigable UI with mocked data | Visual credibility established |
| Week 8 | End-to-end flow working | Integration proof complete |
| Week 10 | Specialist validation passed | Domain credibility confirmed |
| Week 12 | Demo-ready with fallbacks | Risk mitigation complete |

**Technical Constraints:**

| Constraint | Impact | Mitigation |
|------------|--------|------------|
| 12-week timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus exclusively on P0 features |
| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Use seed data, skip authentication |
| Python solver requirement | Cross-language integration | JSON REST API between services |
| OR-Tools learning curve | Development complexity | Simplify model, use greedy fallback |

**Key Assumptions:**

1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support required
4. English UI only (no internationalization)
5. Desktop-primary, tablet-secondary (no mobile optimization)

---

## Chapter 2: Railway Domain Fundamentals

### 2.1 Essential Glossary

All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.

**Core Terms:**

| Term | Definition | NOT Acceptable |
|------|------------|----------------|
| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |

**Maintenance Terms:**

| Term | Definition | Typical Interval |
|------|------------|------------------|
| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |

**Operational Terms:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |

- One Fleet has many Trains (1:N)

### 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)

These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.

**Hard Constraints (Never Violate):**

1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization

**Soft Constraints (Optimize Within):**

1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)

**Critical Timing Rules:**

| Rule | Specification |
|------|---------------|
| PM1 tolerance window | Can be Вұ500 km from trigger |
| PM2 tolerance window | Can be Вұ1,000 km from trigger |
| HM tolerance window | Can be Вұ2,000 km from trigger |
| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |

### 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

Explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and set correct expectations:

**Phase 2+ (Not in Demo):**

| Feature | Reason for Exclusion | Target Phase |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| MILP long-term optimization | Exceeds complexity budget | Phase 2 |
| Real EAM system integration | Requires client environment | Phase 2 |
| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |

**Explicitly NOT Goals:**

- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
- This is NOT a timetabling system
- This is NOT a revenue management system
- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems

---

## Chapter 3: Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning

### 3.1 Competitor Landscape

**Primary Competitors:**

| Competitor | Focus Area | Strength | Weakness |
|------------|------------|----------|----------|
| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |

### 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis

SignatureRail represents the most direct competitor in terms of user experience and feature set.

**SignatureRail Strengths:**

- Clean, modern enterprise UI
- Well-organized workflow panels
- Good use of data tables
- Professional color scheme
- Clear navigation structure

**SignatureRail Weaknesses (MMS Opportunities):**

1. **Basic km tracking without optimization** - Shows variance but doesn't recommend actions
2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios
4. **Separate maintenance and operations** - Silos remain
5. **No map visualization** - Spatial context missing

### 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities

**Gap Analysis:**

| Gap | Market Need | MMS Solution |
|-----|-------------|--------------|
| Passive vs Active Balancing | Systems show variance but don't optimize | AI-powered recommendations with justifications |
| Black Box Decisions | Users don't understand why system recommends | Transparent 3+ reason justifications |
| Operations/Maintenance Silos | Separate systems, manual coordination | Unified interface for both departments |
| Strategic Planning | Day-to-day focus, no simulation | What-if scenarios for planning |
| Dead Running Visibility | Hidden cost, rarely tracked | Explicit display and optimization |

### 3.4 MMS Differentiators

**Primary Differentiators:**

1. **Km Balancing as Core Objective**
   - Not a secondary feature or afterthought
   - Drives all allocation recommendations
   - Visible in dashboard KPIs

2. **Transparent Justifications**
   - Every recommendation shows 3+ reasons
   - Human-readable explanations
   - Builds trust vs black box

3. **Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations**
   - Single interface for both departments
   - Recommendations consider maintenance schedule
   - Reduces coordination overhead

4. **Accessible Simulation**
   - Decision-makers can test scenarios
   - Side-by-side comparison
   - No technical expertise required

5. **Visible Dead Running**
   - Hidden cost made explicit
   - Factored into optimization
   - ROI clearly demonstrable

---

## Chapter 4: Demo Strategy & Presentation

### 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth

> **Mantra:** "Solving 1 pain point extremely well > solving 10 pain points poorly"

**Strategic Focus:**

The demo should demonstrate deep understanding of the km balancing problem rather than showcase many features superficially. Every feature shown must:

1. Be 100% functional (no "coming soon")
2. Use correct railway terminology
3. Show real (simulated but realistic) data
4. Complete end-to-end without errors

### 4.2 Wow Moments

These are specific moments in the demo designed to create impact:

**Wow Moment 1: The Variance Discovery**
- Dashboard loads showing 18% variance (yellow warning)
- Client recognizes their exact problem
- "They're showing us what we live every day"

**Wow Moment 2: The Justification Card**
- Recommendation shows not just WHAT but WHY
- 3+ human-readable reasons
- "Finally, a system that explains itself"

**Wow Moment 3: The What-If**
- Create scenario, adjust allocations
- See projected impact in < 10 seconds
- "I can test my ideas before committing"

**Wow Moment 4: The Map Context**
- See where all trains are spatially
- Click train, see km delta immediately
- "This gives us operational awareness"

### 4.3 Credibility Checklist

Before demo, verify:

- [ ] All terminology is EN 15380 compliant
- [ ] Depot names are real Portuguese locations
- [ ] Service codes follow realistic patterns (IC, R, U)
- [ ] Maintenance intervals are realistic (PM1: 15k km)
- [ ] Train IDs follow realistic naming (UTE-3400-xxx)
- [ ] Km values are in plausible ranges (30k-60k)
- [ ] Variance percentages are realistic (10-25%)
- [ ] Map shows Portugal correctly positioned

### 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid

**NEVER do these in demo:**

| Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Alternative |
|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Show stub screens | Destroys credibility | Show fewer, complete features |
| Use placeholder data | "Lorem ipsum" is unprofessional | Use realistic railway data |
| Demo features that don't work | Client will try to break it | Only show stable features |
| Use generic software terms | "Vehicle" instead of "Train" | Use railway terminology |
| Show loading spinners | Suggests slow system | Pre-cache or mock fast responses |
| Apologize for missing features | Shows lack of confidence | Present what exists as intentional |

### 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)

**Minutes 0-2: Context Setting**
- "We understand your km balancing challenge"
- Show dashboard with variance indicator
- "This is what you see every day"

**Minutes 2-5: The Problem Quantified**
- Click variance to see distribution
- Show outliers (5 over-running, 4 under-running)
- "These 9 trains are costing you money"

**Minutes 5-8: The Solution in Action**
- Navigate to Allocation
- Select a pending service
- Show recommendations with justifications
- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
- Confirm allocation with one click

**Minutes 8-11: Planning Ahead**
- Navigate to Simulation
- Create what-if scenario
- Adjust allocations for under-running trains
- Show projected variance improvement
- "Test your strategy before committing"

**Minutes 11-13: The Big Picture**
- Return to Dashboard
- Show map with train positions
- Show depot capacity
- "Everything in one view"

**Minutes 13-15: Q&A Buffer**
- Prepared questions if no questions asked
- Have fallback screens ready

### 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans

**Anticipated Questions:**

| Question | Prepared Answer |
|----------|-----------------|
| "How does the solver work?" | "CP-SAT optimization balancing multiple constraints - we can show the logic" |
| "Can it integrate with our EAM?" | "Yes, REST API design allows integration with any EAM system" |
| "What about real-time GPS?" | "Architecture supports real-time feeds, demo uses simulated positions" |
| "How long to implement?" | "Core system in 3 months, customization depends on requirements" |
| "What if solver times out?" | "Fallback to simpler ranking ensures always having recommendations" |

**Fallback Plans:**

| Failure | Fallback |
|---------|----------|
| Solver timeout | Pre-computed recommendations in database |
| Database connection lost | Static JSON fallback data |
| Map fails to load | Table view of train positions |
| Browser crash | Second laptop with same setup |
| Network issues | Everything runs locally |

---

lines: +**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.
+Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:
+1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
+2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
+3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints
+MMS addresses all three pain points through:
+- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
+- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
+- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
+**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.
+> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**
+| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
+| Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
+| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
+| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
+| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |
+| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
+| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
+| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
+| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
+| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
+| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
+| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |
+| Demo Stability | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures |
+| Domain Terminology | 100% correct EN 15380 railway terms |
+| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" |
+| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
+| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in live demo |
+| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Maintain interactive feel |
+| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Critical for presentation |
+| Week 4 | Core solver working | Proof of technical capability |
+| Week 6 | Navigable UI with mocked data | Visual credibility established |
+| Week 8 | End-to-end flow working | Integration proof complete |
+| Week 10 | Specialist validation passed | Domain credibility confirmed |
+| Week 12 | Demo-ready with fallbacks | Risk mitigation complete |
+| 12-week timeline | Limited feature scope | Focus exclusively on P0 features |
+| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Use seed data, skip authentication |

+1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
+2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
+All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.
+| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
+| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
+| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
+| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
+| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
+| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
+| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
+| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |
+| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
+| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
+| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
+| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |
+| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
+| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
+| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
+| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
+| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |

+- One Train belongs to one Fleet (N:1)

+These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.
+1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
+2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
+3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
+4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
+5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization
+1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
+2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
+3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
+4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)
+| PM1 tolerance window | Can be Вұ500 km from trigger |
+| PM2 tolerance window | Can be Вұ1,000 km from trigger |
+| HM tolerance window | Can be Вұ2,000 km from trigger |
+| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
+| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |

+| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
+| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
+| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
+| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
+| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
+| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |
+- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
+- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
+- This is NOT a timetabling system
+- This is NOT a revenue management system
+- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
+- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems
+| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
+| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
+| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
+| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |

+2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
+3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios

+- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
+- Prepared questions if no questions asked
+| "How does the solver work?" | "CP-SAT optimization balancing multiple constraints - we can show the logic" |
+| "Can it integrate with our EAM?" | "Yes, REST API design allows integration with any EAM system" |
+| "What about real-time GPS?" | "Architecture supports real-time feeds, demo uses simulated positions" |
+| "How long to implement?" | "Core system in 3 months, customization depends on requirements" |
+| "What if solver times out?" | "Fallback to simpler ranking ensures always having recommendations" |
+| Solver timeout | Pre-computed recommendations in database |
+| Database connection lost | Static JSON fallback data |
+| Map fails to load | Table view of train positions |
+| Browser crash | Second laptop with same setup |

input.new_string:
| Network issues | Everything runs locally |

---

# PART II: USER EXPERIENCE & DESIGN

---

## Chapter 5: User Personas

### 5.1 Paul - The Maintenance Planner

**Profile:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Paul |
| Age | 45 years |
| Role | Maintenance Planner |
| Experience | 18 years in railway maintenance |
| Education | Mechanical Engineering degree |

**Work Context:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Daily System Usage | 4-8 hours |
| Primary Device | Desktop with large monitor (27"+) |
| Secondary Device | Tablet for depot floor checks |
| Work Hours | 07:00 - 16:00 (day shift) |
| Team Size | Reports to Maintenance Manager, works with 3 other planners |

**Goals:**

1. Schedule preventive maintenance minimizing operational impact
2. Balance depot capacity across weeks
3. Ensure no train exceeds maintenance tolerance windows
4. Coordinate with Operations to minimize service disruption
5. Reduce dead running for maintenance movements

**Frustrations:**

1. "I spend hours in Excel trying to balance the schedule"
2. "Operations changes plans without telling me"
3. "I can't see impact of changes until it's too late"
4. "Every depot is managed separately, no fleet-wide view"
5. "Constant firefighting because maintenance gets pushed"

**Success Moment:**
> "I can see the whole fleet's km distribution and immediately know which trains need attention."

**Critical Need:** Gantt chart with drag-drop functionality showing depot capacity

### 5.2 Diana - The Dispatcher

**Profile:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Diana |
| Age | 38 years |
| Role | Dispatcher (Operations Controller) |
| Experience | 12 years in railway operations |
| Education | Transportation Management diploma |

**Work Context:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Daily System Usage | Continuous during 8-hour shift |
| Primary Device | Desktop with multiple monitors (2-3) |
| Secondary Device | Radio/phone for depot communication |
| Work Hours | Rotating shifts (06:00-14:00, 14:00-22:00, 22:00-06:00) |
| Team Size | 2-3 dispatchers per shift |

**Goals:**

1. Allocate trains to services as quickly as possible
2. Minimize service delays due to train unavailability
3. Make good decisions with limited information
4. Handle disruptions efficiently
5. Communicate clearly with depot staff

**Frustrations:**

1. "The system takes forever to show me available trains"
2. "I don't know why one train is better than another"
3. "I have to call depots to check if trains are actually available"
4. "When something goes wrong, I'm blamed for poor train choice"
5. "No time to analyze, just react"

**Success Moment:**
> "The system told me exactly why this train was recommended, so I can defend my decision."

**Critical Need:** Response time < 3 seconds, clear justifications

### 5.3 Gabriel - The Fleet Manager

**Profile:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Name | Gabriel |
| Age | 52 years |
| Role | Fleet Manager |
| Experience | 25 years in railway industry |
| Education | MBA, Engineering background |

**Work Context:**

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Weekly System Usage | 30-60 minutes |
| Primary Device | Desktop or tablet |
| Secondary Device | Mobile phone for alerts |
| Meeting Load | Heavy - needs quick data access |
| Reports To | Operations Director |

**Goals:**

1. Monitor fleet KPIs for board reporting
2. Make strategic decisions on fleet utilization
3. Justify maintenance budgets with data
4. Identify trends before they become problems
5. Benchmark performance across depots

**Frustrations:**

1. "Data is scattered across 5 different systems"
2. "I need my analysts to build every report"
3. "Can't drill down from summary to details"
4. "Numbers don't match between systems"
5. "Takes days to answer simple board questions"

**Success Moment:**
> "I walked into the board meeting and had all the answers at my fingertips."

**Critical Need:** Dashboard with executive view, no navigation required

### 5.4 Design Implications per Persona

**Paul (Planner) Design Requirements:**

| Need | Design Solution |
|------|-----------------|
| Long sessions (4-8h) | Dark mode to reduce eye strain |
| Complex data | Dense information display, data tables |
| Frequent actions | Keyboard shortcuts for power users |
| Pattern recognition | Visual indicators, color coding |
| Planning horizon | Timeline views (Gantt), calendar integration |
| Depot context | Capacity heatmaps, utilization charts |

**Diana (Dispatcher) Design Requirements:**

| Need | Design Solution |
|------|-----------------|
| Speed critical | Instant responses (< 3s) |
| Quick decisions | Single-click actions, confirmation shortcuts |
| Spatial awareness | Map always visible |
| Trust in recommendations | Visible justifications |
| Shift handover | Status at a glance, no hidden states |
| Error recovery | Undo actions, clear error messages |

**Gabriel (Manager) Design Requirements:**

| Need | Design Solution |
|------|-----------------|
| Limited time | KPIs front and center |
| Big picture | Aggregated metrics, trends |
| Touch friendly | Large tap targets for tablet |
| Presentation ready | Clean visuals, exportable |
| Drill down | Click any number for details |
| Mobile alerts | Responsive design, notification support |

## Chapter 6: User Journeys

### 6.1 Journey 1: Allocating a Train to Service (Diana)

**Context:**
Diana arrives at the operations center for her morning shift. The timetable shows that service IC 501 (Lisbon to Porto, 336 km) needs a train assigned for tomorrow's departure.

**Scene 1: The Request**

Diana opens MMS and navigates to the Allocation screen. She selects tomorrow's date using the date picker.

*Screen State:*
- Left panel: List of pending services for selected date
- IC 501 highlighted: "Lisbon вҶ’ Porto | 336 km | Dep: 08:15"
- Status: Unassigned (red indicator)

**Scene 2: Service Selection**

Diana clicks on IC 501. The service details panel expands showing:
- Route: Lisbon Santa ApolГіnia вҶ’ Porto CampanhГЈ
- Distance: 336 km
- Departure: 08:15
- Arrival: 11:45
- Required aptitude: North Line (220 km/h capable)

*Action:* Diana clicks "Get Recommendations"

**Scene 3: The Recommendation**

Within 3 seconds, MMS presents a ranked list of 5 suitable trains.

*Screen State:*
```

в”Ӯ 2. UTE-3400-008                          Score: 87/100      в”Ӯ

в”Ӯ    в”ңв”Җв”Җ вң— Dead running 25km from current location            в”Ӯ

**Scene 4: The Decision**

Diana reviews the justifications. The first recommendation explains exactly WHY this train is best:
- It's under-running (helps balance fleet)
- It ends near Porto depot (efficient for next service or maintenance)
- PM1 is coming up, and Porto has a slot (maintenance coordination)

Diana clicks "Assign" on UTE-3400-012.

**Scene 5: Confirmation**

*Feedback:*
- Success toast: "Train UTE-3400-012 assigned to IC 501"
- Service status changes from red to green
- Map updates showing train's planned route

**Journey Metrics:**
- Total time: < 2 minutes
- Total clicks: 4 (Date вҶ’ Service вҶ’ Recommend вҶ’ Assign)
- Decisions made: 1 (which train)
- Information provided: 4 justifications

**Requirements Revealed:**
- FR-3.1: List pending services
- FR-3.2: Generate train recommendations
- FR-3.3: Display justifications
- FR-3.4: Confirm allocation
- NFR-1.1: < 30 seconds for recommendations
- NFR-4.1: < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks

**Scene 6: The Decision**

Paul sees the trade-off: variance improves 4%, but dead running increases slightly. He decides the trade-off is acceptable.

Paul confirms. Changes are applied.

### 7.2 Drill-Down Hierarchy

**Information Flow:**

```
Level 1: Fleet Summary (Dashboard)
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Click KPI вҶ’ Level 2
    в”Ӯ
Level 2: Segment View (Filtered Lists)
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Click Item вҶ’ Level 3
    в”Ӯ
Level 3: Entity Details (Train, Depot, Service)
    в”Ӯ
    в”ңв”Җв”Җ Click History вҶ’ Level 4
    в”Ӯ
Level 4: Historical Data (Charts, Logs)
```

**Example Drill Paths:**

| Start | Click | Destination |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| Dashboard: Total Trains | KPI card | Fleet list (all) |
| Dashboard: Available | KPI card | Fleet list (filter: fit) |
| Dashboard: In Maintenance | KPI card | Schedule (today) |
| Dashboard: Variance | KPI card | Analysis (distribution) |
| Dashboard: Depot Card | Depot name | Fleet list (filter: depot) |
| Fleet List: Train Row | Train ID | Train Details page |
| Analysis: Outlier | Train ID | Train Details page |
| Schedule: Block | Maintenance event | Event details modal |

### 7.3 Screen Inventory

| Screen | URL | Purpose | Components |
|--------|-----|---------|------------|
| Dashboard | `/` | Executive overview | KPICards, Map, DepotCards, Alerts |
| Fleet List | `/fleet` | Train inventory | DataTable, Filters, Export |
| Train Details | `/fleet/[id]` | Single train view | Tabs, Charts, Timeline |
| Allocation | `/allocation` | Assign trains | ServiceList, Recommendations |
| Analysis | `/analysis` | Balancing + Simulation | Histogram, Outliers, Scenarios |
| Schedule | `/schedule` | Maintenance planning | Gantt, DepotHeatmap |
