#### 17.3 State Management (@zazcart/toolkit)

**Store Structure**

```typescript
// stores/fleetStore.ts
import { createMachine, useMachine, useSelector } from '@zazcart/toolkit';

interface FleetState {
  trains: Train[];
  depots: Depot[];
  selectedTrainId: string | null;
  loading: boolean;
  error: string | null;
}

type FleetEvent =
  | { type: 'FETCH_TRAINS' }
  | { type: 'FETCH_SUCCESS'; trains: Train[] }
  | { type: 'FETCH_ERROR'; error: string }
  | { type: 'SELECT_TRAIN'; trainId: string };

const fleetMachine = createMachine<FleetState, FleetEvent>({
  initial: 'idle',
  context: {
    trains: [],
    depots: [],
    selectedTrainId: null,
    loading: false,
    error: null,
  },
  states: {
    idle: {
      on: {
        FETCH_TRAINS: {
          target: 'loading',
          actions: ['setLoading'],
        },
      },
    },
    loading: {
      on: {
        FETCH_SUCCESS: {
          target: 'idle',
          actions: ['setTrains', 'clearLoading'],
        },
        FETCH_ERROR: {
          target: 'error',
          actions: ['setError'],
        },
      },
    },
    error: {
      on: {
        FETCH_TRAINS: {
          target: 'loading',
          actions: ['setLoading', 'clearError'],
        },
      },
    },
  },
});

// Selectors
export const selectTrains = (state: FleetState) => state.trains;
export const selectSelectedTrain = (state: FleetState) =>
  state.trains.find(t => t.id === state.selectedTrainId);

// Hook
export function useFleetStore() {
  return useMachine(fleetMachine);
}
```

**Usage in Components**

```typescript
// components/fleet/TrainList.tsx
import { useFleetStore, selectTrains } from '@/stores/fleetStore';

export function TrainList() {
  const { state, send } = useFleetStore();
  const trains = useSelector(selectTrains);

  useEffect(() => {
    send({ type: 'FETCH_TRAINS' });
  }, [send]);

  if (state.loading) return <Spinner />;
  if (state.error) return <ErrorMessage error={state.error} />;

  return (
    <div>
      {trains.map(train => (
        <TrainCard
          key={train.id}
          train={train}
          onClick={() => send({ type: 'SELECT_TRAIN', trainId: train.id })}
        />
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}
```

#### 17.4 API Client Pattern

**Centralized API Client**

```typescript
// lib/api.ts
const API_BASE = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL || 'http://localhost:4000/api';

interface ApiResponse<T> {
  data: T;
  meta?: {
    total?: number;
    page?: number;
  };
}

interface ApiError {
  error: string;
  code: string;
  details?: Record<string, unknown>;
}

async function request<T>(
  endpoint: string,
  options: RequestInit = {}
): Promise<T> {
  const url = `${API_BASE}${endpoint}`;

  const response = await fetch(url, {
    ...options,
    headers: {
      'Content-Type': 'application/json',
      ...options.headers,
    },
  });

  if (!response.ok) {
    const error: ApiError = await response.json();
    throw new Error(error.error || 'API Error');
  }

  const result: ApiResponse<T> = await response.json();
  return result.data;
}

// Typed API methods
export const api = {
  // Dashboard
  getDashboardSummary: () =>
    request<DashboardSummary>('/dashboard/summary'),

  getDepotCapacity: (date?: string) =>
    request<DepotCapacity[]>(`/dashboard/depot-capacity${date ? `?date=${date}` : ''}`),

  // Trains
  getTrains: (params?: TrainFilters) =>
    request<Train[]>(`/trains${toQueryString(params)}`),

  getTrain: (id: string) =>
    request<Train>(`/trains/${id}`),

  getTrainHistory: (id: string, months?: number) =>
    request<MileagePoint[]>(`/trains/${id}/history?months=${months || 6}`),

  // Services & Allocation
  getPendingServices: (date: string) =>
    request<Service[]>(`/services/pending?date=${date}`),

  getRecommendations: (serviceId: string, date: string) =>
    request<Recommendation[]>('/allocations/recommend', {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify({ serviceId, date }),
    }),

  createAllocation: (data: AllocationRequest) =>
    request<Allocation>('/allocations', {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify(data),
    }),

  // Balancing
  getDistribution: (fleetId?: string) =>
    request<DistributionData>(`/balancing/distribution${fleetId ? `?fleet=${fleetId}` : ''}`),

  getOutliers: () =>
    request<OutliersData>('/balancing/outliers'),

  // Simulation
  createScenario: (name: string) =>
    request<Scenario>('/scenarios', {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify({ name }),
    }),

  runSimulation: (scenarioId: string, adjustments: Adjustment[]) =>
    request<SimulationResult>(`/scenarios/${scenarioId}/simulate`, {
      method: 'POST',
      body: JSON.stringify({ adjustments }),
    }),

  // Maintenance
  getMaintenanceSchedule: (startDate: string, endDate: string) =>
    request<MaintenanceBlock[]>(`/maintenance?start=${startDate}&end=${endDate}`),

  rescheduleMaintenace: (id: string, newDate: string) =>
    request<MaintenanceBlock>(`/maintenance/${id}`, {
      method: 'PATCH',
      body: JSON.stringify({ scheduledDate: newDate }),
    }),
};
```

```typescript
// Using Blueprint.js components
import {
  Card,
  Elevation,
  Button,
  Intent,
  Tag,
  Spinner,
  NonIdealState,
  Tooltip,
} from '@blueprintjs/core';

export function KPICard({ title, value, status, onClick }: KPICardProps) {
  return (
    <Card
      elevation={Elevation.TWO}
      interactive={!!onClick}
      onClick={onClick}
      className="kpi-card"
    >
      <div className="kpi-header">
        <span className="kpi-title">{title}</span>
        <Tag intent={statusToIntent(status)}>{status}</Tag>
      </div>
      <div className="kpi-value">{value}</div>
    </Card>
  );
}

function statusToIntent(status?: string): Intent {
  switch (status) {
    case 'green': return Intent.SUCCESS;
    case 'yellow': return Intent.WARNING;
    case 'red': return Intent.DANGER;
    default: return Intent.NONE;
  }
}
```

### Chapter 18: Backend Architecture

#### 18.1 Fastify Server Structure

```typescript
// apps/api/src/index.ts
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import cors from '@fastify/cors';
import { dashboardRoutes } from './routes/dashboard';
import { trainRoutes } from './routes/trains';
import { allocationRoutes } from './routes/allocations';
import { simulationRoutes } from './routes/simulation';
import { maintenanceRoutes } from './routes/maintenance';
import { db } from './db/client';

const server = Fastify({
  logger: true,
});

// Plugins
await server.register(cors, {
  origin: ['http://localhost:3000'],
});

// Database connection
server.decorate('db', db);

// Routes
server.register(dashboardRoutes, { prefix: '/api/dashboard' });
server.register(trainRoutes, { prefix: '/api/trains' });
server.register(allocationRoutes, { prefix: '/api/allocations' });
server.register(simulationRoutes, { prefix: '/api/simulation' });
server.register(maintenanceRoutes, { prefix: '/api/maintenance' });

// Health check
server.get('/health', async () => ({ status: 'ok' }));

// Start server
const start = async () => {
  try {
    await server.listen({ port: 4000, host: '0.0.0.0' });
    console.log('Server running on http://localhost:4000');
  } catch (err) {
    server.log.error(err);
    process.exit(1);
  }
};

start();
```

#### 18.2 Route Organization

**Route File Pattern**

```typescript
// apps/api/src/routes/trains.ts
import { FastifyPluginAsync } from 'fastify';
import { TrainService } from '../services/trainService';
import { TrainFilters, TrainResponse } from '../types';

export const trainRoutes: FastifyPluginAsync = async (fastify) => {
  const trainService = new TrainService(fastify.db);

  // GET /api/trains
  fastify.get<{
    Querystring: TrainFilters;
    Reply: { data: TrainResponse[] };
  }>('/', {
    schema: {
      querystring: {
        type: 'object',
        properties: {
          fleet: { type: 'string' },
          depot: { type: 'string' },
          status: { type: 'string', enum: ['fit', 'unfit'] },
        },
      },
    },
  }, async (request, reply) => {
    const trains = await trainService.getTrains(request.query);
    return { data: trains };
  });

  // GET /api/trains/:id
  fastify.get<{
    Params: { id: string };
    Reply: { data: TrainResponse };
  }>('/:id', async (request, reply) => {
    const train = await trainService.getTrainById(request.params.id);
    if (!train) {
      return reply.status(404).send({ error: 'Train not found', code: 'NOT_FOUND' });
    }
    return { data: train };
  });

  // GET /api/trains/:id/history
  fastify.get<{
    Params: { id: string };
    Querystring: { months?: number };
    Reply: { data: MileagePoint[] };
  }>('/:id/history', async (request, reply) => {
    const months = request.query.months || 6;
    const history = await trainService.getTrainHistory(request.params.id, months);
    return { data: history };
  });
};
```

**Route Summary**

| Method | Path | Handler | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| GET | /api/dashboard/summary | getDashboardSummary | KPI metrics |
| GET | /api/dashboard/depot-capacity | getDepotCapacity | Depot utilization |
| GET | /api/trains | getTrains | List trains with filters |
| GET | /api/trains/:id | getTrainById | Single train details |
| GET | /api/trains/:id/history | getTrainHistory | Mileage history |
| GET | /api/services/pending | getPendingServices | Unallocated services |
| POST | /api/allocations/recommend | getRecommendations | Solver recommendations |
| POST | /api/allocations | createAllocation | Create allocation |
| GET | /api/balancing/distribution | getDistribution | Km distribution |
| GET | /api/balancing/outliers | getOutliers | Over/under runners |
| POST | /api/scenarios | createScenario | New scenario |
| POST | /api/scenarios/:id/simulate | runSimulation | Execute simulation |
| GET | /api/maintenance | getSchedule | Maintenance timeline |
| PATCH | /api/maintenance/:id | reschedule | Move maintenance |

#### 18.3 Service Layer Pattern

**Service Class Structure**

```typescript
// apps/api/src/services/trainService.ts
import { eq, and, gte, lte, sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
import { db } from '../db/client';
import { trains, fleets, depots, mileageHistory } from '../db/schema';

export class TrainService {
  constructor(private db: typeof db) {}

  async getTrains(filters: TrainFilters): Promise<TrainResponse[]> {
    let query = this.db
      .select({
        id: trains.id,
        trainId: trains.trainId,
        currentKm: trains.currentKm,
        fitnessStatus: trains.fitnessStatus,
        fleetName: fleets.name,
        depotName: depots.name,
        latitude: trains.latitude,
        longitude: trains.longitude,
      })
      .from(trains)
      .leftJoin(fleets, eq(trains.fleetId, fleets.id))
      .leftJoin(depots, eq(trains.baseDepotId, depots.id));

    if (filters.fleet) {
      query = query.where(eq(trains.fleetId, filters.fleet));
    }
    if (filters.depot) {
      query = query.where(eq(trains.baseDepotId, filters.depot));
    }
    if (filters.status) {
      query = query.where(eq(trains.fitnessStatus, filters.status));
    }

    return query;
  }

  async getTrainById(id: string): Promise<TrainDetailResponse | null> {
    const [train] = await this.db
      .select()
      .from(trains)
      .leftJoin(fleets, eq(trains.fleetId, fleets.id))
      .leftJoin(depots, eq(trains.baseDepotId, depots.id))
      .where(eq(trains.id, id));

    if (!train) return null;

    // Calculate delta from fleet average
    const avgResult = await this.db
      .select({ avg: sql<number>`avg(${trains.currentKm})` })
      .from(trains)
      .where(eq(trains.fleetId, train.trains.fleetId));

    const fleetAverage = avgResult[0]?.avg || 0;
    const deltaPercent = ((train.trains.currentKm - fleetAverage) / fleetAverage) * 100;

    return {
      ...train.trains,
      fleet: train.fleets,
      depot: train.depots,
      deltaPercent: Math.round(deltaPercent * 10) / 10,
    };
  }

  async getTrainHistory(trainId: string, months: number): Promise<MileagePoint[]> {
    const startDate = new Date();
    startDate.setMonth(startDate.getMonth() - months);

    return this.db
      .select({
        date: mileageHistory.recordedDate,
        km: mileageHistory.kmValue,
      })
      .from(mileageHistory)
      .where(
        and(
          eq(mileageHistory.trainId, trainId),
          gte(mileageHistory.recordedDate, startDate)
        )
      )
      .orderBy(mileageHistory.recordedDate);
  }
}
```

#### 18.4 Database Access (Drizzle ORM)

**Schema Definition**

```typescript
// apps/api/src/db/schema.ts
import {
  pgTable,
  uuid,
  varchar,
  integer,
  decimal,
  timestamp,
  date,
  time,
  jsonb,
  index,
  uniqueIndex,
} from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';

export const fleets = pgTable('fleets', {
  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
  name: varchar('name', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
  manufacturer: varchar('manufacturer', { length: 50 }),
  pm1IntervalKm: integer('pm1_interval_km').notNull(),
  pm2IntervalKm: integer('pm2_interval_km').notNull(),
  hmIntervalKm: integer('hm_interval_km').notNull(),
  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
});

export const depots = pgTable('depots', {
  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
  name: varchar('name', { length: 100 }).notNull(),
  code: varchar('code', { length: 10 }).notNull(),
  latitude: decimal('latitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
  longitude: decimal('longitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
  capacityTracks: integer('capacity_tracks').default(5),
  capacityManhours: integer('capacity_manhours').default(80),
  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
}, (table) => [
  uniqueIndex('depots_code_idx').on(table.code),
]);

export const trains = pgTable('trains', {
  id: uuid('id').primaryKey().defaultRandom(),
  trainId: varchar('train_id', { length: 20 }).notNull(),
  fleetId: uuid('fleet_id').references(() => fleets.id),
  baseDepotId: uuid('base_depot_id').references(() => depots.id),
  currentKm: integer('current_km').default(0),
  targetKm: integer('target_km').default(0),
  fitnessStatus: varchar('fitness_status', { length: 10 }).default('fit'),
  lastMaintenanceDate: date('last_maintenance_date'),
  nextMaintenanceKm: integer('next_maintenance_km'),
  latitude: decimal('latitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
  longitude: decimal('longitude', { precision: 10, scale: 6 }),
  createdAt: timestamp('created_at').defaultNow(),
  updatedAt: timestamp('updated_at').defaultNow(),
}, (table) => [
  uniqueIndex('trains_train_id_idx').on(table.trainId),
  index('trains_fleet_idx').on(table.fleetId),
  index('trains_depot_idx').on(table.baseDepotId),
]);

// ... additional tables (services, allocations, mileageHistory, maintenanceSchedules)
```

#### 18.5 Caching Strategy (Redis)

**Cache Configuration**

```typescript
// apps/api/src/cache/redis.ts
import Redis from 'ioredis';

const redis = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL || 'redis://localhost:6379');

export const cache = {
  async get<T>(key: string): Promise<T | null> {
    const data = await redis.get(key);
    return data ? JSON.parse(data) : null;
  },

  async set<T>(key: string, value: T, ttlSeconds: number): Promise<void> {
    await redis.setex(key, ttlSeconds, JSON.stringify(value));
  },

  async del(key: string): Promise<void> {
    await redis.del(key);
  },

  async invalidatePattern(pattern: string): Promise<void> {
    const keys = await redis.keys(pattern);
    if (keys.length > 0) {
      await redis.del(...keys);
    }
  },
};

// Cache key patterns
export const CACHE_KEYS = {
  DASHBOARD_SUMMARY: 'dashboard:summary',
  DEPOT_CAPACITY: (date: string) => `depot:capacity:${date}`,
  TRAINS_LIST: (hash: string) => `trains:list:${hash}`,
  TRAIN_DETAIL: (id: string) => `train:${id}`,
  SOLVER_RESULT: (hash: string) => `solver:${hash}`,
};

// TTL values in seconds
export const CACHE_TTL = {
  DASHBOARD: 60,      // 1 minute
  TRAINS: 30,         // 30 seconds
  DEPOT: 300,         // 5 minutes
  SOLVER: 300,        // 5 minutes
  STATIC: 3600,       // 1 hour
};
```

**Cache Usage in Service**

```typescript
// Using cache in service layer
async getDashboardSummary(): Promise<DashboardSummary> {
  // Try cache first
  const cached = await cache.get<DashboardSummary>(CACHE_KEYS.DASHBOARD_SUMMARY);
  if (cached) return cached;

  // Fetch from database
  const summary = await this.computeDashboardSummary();

  // Store in cache
  await cache.set(CACHE_KEYS.DASHBOARD_SUMMARY, summary, CACHE_TTL.DASHBOARD);

  return summary;
}
```

**Global Error Handler**

```typescript
// Error handler plugin
fastify.setErrorHandler((error, request, reply) => {
  if (error instanceof AppError) {
    return reply.status(error.statusCode).send({
      error: error.message,
      code: error.code,
      details: error.details,
    });
  }

  // Unexpected error
  fastify.log.error(error);
  return reply.status(500).send({
    error: 'Internal Server Error',
    code: 'INTERNAL_ERROR',
  });
});
```

### Chapter 19: Python Solver Architecture

This chapter details the OR-Tools optimization engine.

#### 19.1 OR-Tools CP-SAT Model

**Solver Structure**

```python
# solver/src/engine/allocator.py
from ortools.sat.python import cp_model
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Tuple

@dataclass
class Train:
    id: str
    train_code: str
    current_km: int
    fleet_average: int
    depot_id: str
    latitude: float
    longitude: float
    fitness_status: str

@dataclass
class Service:
    id: str
    service_code: str
    distance_km: int
    origin: str
    destination: str
    origin_lat: float
    origin_lng: float
    dest_lat: float
    dest_lng: float

class AllocationSolver:
    def __init__(self, trains: List[Train], service: Service, config: dict):
        self.trains = trains
        self.service = service
        self.config = config
        self.model = cp_model.CpModel()

    def solve(self) -> List[dict]:
        # Filter fit trains only
        candidates = [t for t in self.trains if t.fitness_status == 'fit']

        if not candidates:
            return []

        # Score each candidate
        scored = []
        for train in candidates:
            score = self._calculate_score(train)
            justifications = self._generate_justifications(train)
            scored.append({
                'train_id': train.id,
                'train_code': train.train_code,
                'score': score,
                'justifications': justifications,
                'dead_running_km': self._calculate_dead_running(train),
            })

        # Sort by score descending
        scored.sort(key=lambda x: x['score'], reverse=True)

        # Return top 5
        return scored[:5]
```

#### 19.2 Scoring Algorithm

**Score Breakdown**

| Factor | Weight | Scoring Logic |
|--------|--------|--------------|
| Km Balance | 40% | Lower delta from average = higher score |
| Dead Running | 15% | Shorter empty km = higher score |
| Depot Proximity | 25% | Closer to maintenance depot = higher score |
| Maintenance Alignment | 20% | Aligned with scheduled maintenance = higher score |

**Score Calculation**

```python
def _calculate_score(self, train: Train) -> int:
    score = 100  # Start with perfect score

    # Factor 1: Km Balance (40% weight)
    delta_pct = abs(train.current_km - train.fleet_average) / train.fleet_average
    balance_penalty = min(delta_pct * 100, 40)

    # Bonus for under-running trains
    if train.current_km < train.fleet_average:
        balance_penalty = balance_penalty * 0.5
    score -= balance_penalty

    # Factor 2: Dead Running (15% weight)
    dead_km = self._calculate_dead_running(train)
    dead_penalty = min((dead_km / 100) * 15, 15)
    score -= dead_penalty

    # Factor 3: Depot Proximity (25% weight)
    dest_to_depot = self._distance_to_nearest_depot(train)
    proximity_penalty = min((dest_to_depot / 50) * 25, 25)
    score -= proximity_penalty

    return max(0, min(100, int(score)))
```

#### 19.3 Justification Generation

**Justification Templates**

```python
JUSTIFICATION_TEMPLATES = {
    'km_balance_under': "Km {delta:.0f}% below fleet average - prioritizing for balance",
    'km_balance_over': "Km {delta:.0f}% above average - acceptable given other factors",
    'dead_running_low': "Only {km}km dead running to service start",
    'dead_running_high': "вҡ пёҸ {km}km dead running - consider alternatives",
    'depot_close': "Service ends {km}km from {depot} maintenance depot",
    'maintenance_aligned': "PM{type} scheduled in {days} days - natural alignment",
}
```

```python
# solver/src/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException

app = FastAPI(title="MMS Solver", version="1.0.0")

@app.post("/recommend", response_model=RecommendResponse)

    start_time = time.time()
    solver = AllocationSolver(...)
    results = solver.solve()
```

#### 19.5 Fallback Mechanisms

**Simple Scoring Fallback**

```python
def simple_score(trains: List[Train], service: Service) -> List[dict]:
    """Fallback when CP-SAT times out."""
    scored = []
    for train in trains:
        if train.fitness_status != 'fit':
            continue
        delta_pct = abs(train.current_km - train.fleet_average) / train.fleet_average
        score = max(0, 100 - int(delta_pct * 100))
        if train.current_km < train.fleet_average:
            score = min(100, score + 10)
        scored.append({
            'train_id': train.id,
            'train_code': train.train_code,
            'score': score,
            'justifications': ["Scored using simple heuristic (solver timeout)"],
            'dead_running_km': 0,
        })
    return sorted(scored, key=lambda x: x['score'], reverse=True)[:5]
```

#### 20.5 Query Patterns

**Dashboard Summary**

```sql
SELECT
  COUNT(*) as total_trains,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE fitness_status = 'fit') as fit_count,
  ROUND(STDDEV(current_km) / AVG(current_km) * 100, 1) as km_variance
FROM trains;
```

**Train Ranking (Outliers)**

```sql
WITH fleet_stats AS (
  SELECT fleet_id, AVG(current_km) as avg, STDDEV(current_km) as stddev
  FROM trains GROUP BY fleet_id
)
SELECT t.train_id, t.current_km,
       ROUND((t.current_km - fs.avg) / fs.avg * 100, 1) as delta_pct
FROM trains t
JOIN fleet_stats fs ON t.fleet_id = fs.fleet_id
ORDER BY ABS(delta_pct) DESC;
```

---

# Part VI: Lessons Learned & Recovery

This part documents lessons learned during the development process, common pitfalls to avoid, and recovery procedures for various failure scenarios.

---

## Chapter 26: Lessons Learned

### 26.1 Architecture Decisions

#### What Worked Well

**1. Monorepo Structure**

Keeping frontend, API, and solver in a single repository provided:
- Atomic commits across system boundaries
- Shared TypeScript types
- Simplified CI/CD pipelines
- Easy local development

**2. Dedicated Python Solver**

Separating the optimization engine brought:
- Access to OR-Tools' mature CP-SAT solver
- Independent scaling of compute-intensive operations
- Clear API contract between services
- Ability to test solver logic in isolation

**3. Drizzle ORM over Prisma**

Drizzle provided advantages for this project:
- Better TypeScript inference
- More control over generated SQL
- Faster migrations in development
- Smaller bundle size

**4. Blueprint.js Component Library**

The enterprise-focused component library offered:
- Dark mode out of the box
- Complex data table components
- Professional look without extensive styling
- Good documentation

#### What Could Be Improved

**1. State Management Complexity**

Using @zazcart/toolkit required a learning curve. For simpler projects, React Query or SWR might suffice. The state machine approach was valuable for complex workflows but added overhead for simple CRUD operations.

**2. Map Integration**

Mapbox GL integration had challenges:
- Large bundle size impact
- Complex TypeScript types
- Performance tuning required for many markers
- Limited offline support

**3. Solver Communication**

HTTP-based solver integration had limitations:
- Timeout handling complexity
- No streaming progress updates
- Cold start latency

Future consideration: WebSocket connection or server-sent events for real-time solver progress.

### 26.2 Development Process

#### Effective Practices

**1. BMAD Methodology**

The Business-Machine-Agent-Development workflow provided:
- Clear separation between planning and implementation
- Documented decision rationale
- Structured story creation
- Consistent development patterns

**2. Visual Testing with Playwright**

Mandatory visual verification caught issues that unit tests missed:
- Layout regressions
- Missing translations
- Console errors not caught by TypeScript
- Performance issues visible through timing

**3. Incremental Commits**

Small, focused commits enabled:
- Easy rollback of problematic changes
- Clear history for debugging
- Better code review
- Atomic feature flags

#### Process Improvements

**1. Earlier Integration Testing**

Waiting too long to test API + frontend integration led to cascading fixes. Recommendation: Integration test after each API endpoint is complete.

**2. Performance Budgets**

Setting explicit performance targets earlier would have prevented late-stage optimization. Recommendation: Define performance budgets in the PRD phase.

**3. Mock Data Realism**

Early mock data was too clean. Real data revealed edge cases:
- Very long train IDs breaking layouts
- Null values in optional fields
- Date timezone issues
- Decimal precision in km calculations

### 26.3 Technical Pitfalls

#### React Patterns

**Problem: Stale Closures in Animations**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - stale closure
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
useEffect(() => {
  const interval = setInterval(() => {
    setCount(count + 1); // Always uses initial count
  }, 1000);
  return () => clearInterval(interval);
}, []);

// вң… CORRECT - functional update
setCount(prev => prev + 1);

// вң… BETTER - use state machine
machine.send('INCREMENT');
```

**Problem: Excessive Re-renders**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - creates new object every render
<Map config={{ center: [lat, lng], zoom: 10 }} />

// вң… CORRECT - memoize config
const mapConfig = useMemo(() => ({
  center: [lat, lng],
  zoom: 10
}), [lat, lng]);
<Map config={mapConfig} />
```

#### API Design

**Problem: Over-fetching Data**

Initial API returned full train objects everywhere. Solution: GraphQL-style field selection or dedicated DTOs per view.

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - returns everything
GET /api/trains
// Response: 45 trains Г— 20 fields Г— relations = massive payload

// вң… CORRECT - return what's needed
GET /api/trains?fields=trainId,currentKm,fitnessStatus
// Or: dedicated endpoint
GET /api/dashboard/fleet-summary
```

**Problem: N+1 Queries**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - N+1 queries
const trains = await db.select().from(trains);
for (const train of trains) {
  train.fleet = await db.select().from(fleets).where(eq(fleets.id, train.fleetId));
}

// вң… CORRECT - single query with join
const trainsWithFleet = await db.query.trains.findMany({
  with: { fleet: true }
});
```

#### Database Design

**Problem: Missing Indexes**

Slow queries on date-range filters. Solution: Add composite indexes for common query patterns.

```sql
-- Before: Full table scan
SELECT * FROM allocations WHERE allocation_date BETWEEN ? AND ?;

-- After: Index scan
CREATE INDEX allocations_date_idx ON allocations(allocation_date);
```

**Problem: UUID Performance**

UUIDs as primary keys had performance implications:
- Larger index size
- Random insertion pattern (non-clustered)
- Harder to debug (can't eyeball IDs)

Mitigation: Use UUIDs for external API, but consider sequential IDs internally.

### 26.4 Domain-Specific Learnings

#### Railway Terminology

| Term We Used | Correct Railway Term |
|--------------|----------------------|
| "Maintenance" | "Works" or "Overhaul" |
| "Train" | "Unit" or "Trainset" |
| "Route" | "Service" or "Diagram" |
| "Driver" | "Train Operator" or "Motorman" |

Recommendation: Involve domain experts in terminology review.

#### Mileage Calculation Nuances

**Distance vs Km Run**

- **Commercial km**: Distance with passengers
- **Dead running km**: Empty movement between locations
- **Total km**: Sum of commercial + dead running

The solver needed to distinguish these for accurate balancing.

**Maintenance Intervals**

Maintenance isn't just about km:
- Calendar-based maintenance (even if low km)
- Component-specific intervals
- Regulatory inspections (time-based)

Future enhancement: Multi-factor maintenance scheduling.

#### Optimization Realities

**Solver Performance vs Quality Trade-off**

| Timeout | Solution Quality | Use Case |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| 5s | 85% optimal | Interactive preview |
| 30s | 95% optimal | Daily planning |
| 5min | 99% optimal | Weekly optimization |

**Constraint Softness**

Hard constraints the solver cannot break:
- Train fitness (unfit cannot run)
- Depot capacity (physical limit)

Soft constraints that can be violated with penalty:
- Balancing preference
- Dead running minimization
- Operator preferences

### 26.5 Team & Communication

#### Documentation Effectiveness

**What Helped**

- Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) explaining "why"
- API examples in documentation
- Video walkthroughs of complex workflows
- Diagram-first communication

**What Didn't Help**

- Overly detailed specifications (became outdated)
- Comments that restated the code
- Documentation in separate wiki (diverged from code)

#### Stakeholder Management

**Effective Practices**

- Weekly demo with working software
- Clear "definition of done" for each sprint
- Visual progress tracking (burndown chart)
- Early feedback on UI mockups

**Challenges**

- Requirements changes mid-sprint
- Different stakeholder priorities
- Technical debt visibility to non-technical stakeholders

### 26.6 Recommendations for Future Projects

1. **Start with constraints**: Define performance budgets, data volumes, and non-functional requirements early

2. **Mock realistic data**: Generate edge cases from day one

3. **Integrate continuously**: Don't wait for "API complete" - test integrations incrementally

4. **Document decisions, not code**: Explain "why" in ADRs, let code explain "what"

5. **Involve domain experts**: Railway operations knowledge is irreplaceable

6. **Plan for demo failures**: Have backup materials ready

7. **Performance test early**: Solver timeout issues are hard to fix late

8. **Accessibility first**: Adding accessibility retroactively is expensive

---

## Chapter 27: Recovery Procedures

### 27.1 Service Recovery Matrix

| Service | Impact | Recovery Time | Recovery Procedure |
|---------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Frontend | UI unavailable | 5 min | Vercel rollback |
| API | No data access | 10 min | Railway rollback + DB check |
| Solver | No recommendations | 15 min | Railway rollback + cache clear |
| Database | Complete outage | 30 min | Point-in-time restore |
| Redis | Degraded performance | 5 min | Redis restart or failover |

### 27.2 Frontend Recovery

#### Symptom: Blank Screen

**Diagnosis Steps**

1. Check browser console for errors
2. Verify API endpoint connectivity
3. Check Vercel deployment status
4. Review recent deployments

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Check deployment status
vercel ls --app mms-web

# 2. Rollback to last working deployment
vercel rollback <last-good-deployment-id>

# 3. If rollback fails, redeploy from known good commit
git checkout <good-commit>
vercel --prod
```

#### Symptom: API Connection Errors

**Diagnosis**

```javascript
// Check in browser console
fetch('/api/health').then(r => r.json()).then(console.log)
```

**Recovery**

1. Verify `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` environment variable
2. Check API service health
3. Verify CORS configuration
4. Check for SSL certificate issues

### 27.3 API Recovery

#### Symptom: 500 Errors

**Diagnosis Steps**

```bash
# 1. Check logs
railway logs --app mms-api

# 2. Check database connection
curl https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/health

# 3. Verify environment variables
railway variables --app mms-api
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# Option 1: Restart service
railway restart --app mms-api

# Option 2: Rollback deployment
railway rollback --app mms-api

# Option 3: Database connection fix
# Check DATABASE_URL is valid
# Test connection manually
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"
```

#### Symptom: Slow Responses

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Check response times
curl -w "@curl-format.txt" -o /dev/null -s https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/trains

# curl-format.txt:
#   time_namelookup:  %{time_namelookup}s
#   time_connect:     %{time_connect}s
#   time_appconnect:  %{time_appconnect}s
#   time_total:       %{time_total}s
```

**Recovery Actions**

1. Scale up Railway instance
2. Check database query performance
3. Clear Redis cache
4. Review recent code changes for N+1 queries

### 27.4 Solver Recovery

#### Symptom: Solver Timeout

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Check solver logs
railway logs --app mms-solver

# Test solver endpoint
curl -X POST https://solver.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/optimize/allocation \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"trains": [], "services": []}'
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Increase timeout limit
railway variables set MAX_SOLVER_TIME=60 --app mms-solver

# 2. Scale up compute
railway scale --app mms-solver --memory 2GB

# 3. Reduce problem size (temporary)
# Implement pagination or batch processing
```

#### Symptom: Solver Returns Infeasible

**Diagnosis**

Check solver response for constraint violations:
- All trains unfit?
- Services exceed fleet capacity?
- Depot capacity too low?

**Recovery Actions**

1. Review input data for invalid values
2. Check for data corruption
3. Verify constraint parameters
4. Implement constraint relaxation fallback

### 27.5 Database Recovery

#### Symptom: Connection Failures

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Test connection
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"

# Check connection pool
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity"
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Restart connection pool
railway restart --app mms-api

# 2. Increase pool size (if exhausted)
# Update DATABASE_URL with ?pool_size=20

# 3. Kill idle connections
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "
  SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
  FROM pg_stat_activity
  WHERE state = 'idle'
  AND query_start < now() - interval '5 minutes';
"
```

#### Symptom: Data Corruption

**Diagnosis**

```sql
-- Check for orphaned records
SELECT t.id FROM trains t
LEFT JOIN fleets f ON t.fleet_id = f.id
WHERE f.id IS NULL AND t.fleet_id IS NOT NULL;

-- Check for invalid values
SELECT * FROM trains WHERE current_km < 0 OR current_km > 10000000;
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Identify corruption scope
# Run integrity checks

# 2. Point-in-time recovery (if needed)
# Contact Neon support for PITR

# 3. Restore from backup
pg_restore -d $DATABASE_URL backup.dump
```

#### Backup Procedures

**Automated Backups**

Neon provides automatic point-in-time recovery. For additional safety:

```bash
# Daily backup script
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

# Upload to cloud storage
aws s3 cp backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql s3://mms-backups/
```

**Restore from Backup**

```bash
# 1. Create new database
neon databases create mms_restored

# 2. Restore data
psql $NEW_DATABASE_URL < backups/mms-20250115.sql

# 3. Update API to use new database
railway variables set DATABASE_URL=$NEW_DATABASE_URL --app mms-api
```

### 27.6 Redis Recovery

#### Symptom: Cache Miss Storm

When Redis fails, all requests hit the database:

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Restart Redis
railway restart --app mms-redis

# 2. Implement graceful degradation
# API should work without cache (slower)

# 3. Warm up cache
curl -X POST https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/cache/warm
```

#### Symptom: Memory Exhaustion

```bash
# Check memory usage
redis-cli INFO memory

# Clear specific patterns
redis-cli KEYS "cache:train:*" | xargs redis-cli DEL

# Set memory policy
redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
```

### 27.7 Data Recovery Procedures

#### Restore Deleted Trains

```sql
-- If soft delete is implemented
UPDATE trains SET deleted_at = NULL WHERE id = '<train-id>';

-- If hard delete, restore from audit log
INSERT INTO trains (id, train_id, fleet_id, current_km, ...)
SELECT id, train_id, fleet_id, current_km, ...
FROM audit_log
WHERE table_name = 'trains'
  AND record_id = '<train-id>'
  AND operation = 'DELETE'
ORDER BY timestamp DESC
LIMIT 1;
```

#### Restore Deleted Allocations

```sql
-- Restore allocations for a specific date
INSERT INTO allocations (service_id, train_id, allocation_date, status)
SELECT service_id, train_id, allocation_date, 'restored'
FROM allocations_backup
WHERE allocation_date = '2025-01-15';
```

#### Fix Incorrect Mileage

```sql
-- Identify incorrect values
SELECT * FROM mileage_history
WHERE km_value > 1000000 OR km_value < 0;

-- Correct using previous valid record
UPDATE mileage_history mh
SET km_value = (
  SELECT km_value
  FROM mileage_history mh2
  WHERE mh2.train_id = mh.train_id
    AND mh2.recorded_date < mh.recorded_date
    AND mh2.km_value BETWEEN 0 AND 1000000
  ORDER BY recorded_date DESC
  LIMIT 1
)
WHERE km_value > 1000000 OR km_value < 0;
```

### 27.8 Disaster Recovery

#### Complete System Failure

**Recovery Steps**

1. **Assess scope** (5 min)
   - Which services are down?
   - Is data intact?
   - What was last known good state?

2. **Communicate** (immediately)
   - Notify stakeholders
   - Update status page
   - Set expectations for recovery time

3. **Database recovery** (30 min)
   - Restore from most recent backup
   - Verify data integrity
   - Update connection strings

4. **Service recovery** (in order)
   - Redis (5 min)
   - Solver (10 min)
   - API (10 min)
   - Frontend (5 min)

5. **Verification** (15 min)
   - Run smoke tests
   - Check critical paths
   - Verify data consistency

6. **Post-incident** (after recovery)
   - Document timeline
   - Identify root cause
   - Implement preventive measures

#### Regional Failover

If primary region (GRU1) is unavailable:

```bash
# 1. Deploy to backup region
vercel deploy --prod --region fra1

# 2. Update DNS
# Change A record to point to backup region

# 3. Update API URL
vercel env add NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL https://api-backup.mms-mfc.railway.app production
```

### 27.9 Incident Response Template

```markdown
## Incident Report: [TITLE]

**Date/Time**: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC
**Duration**: X hours Y minutes
**Severity**: Critical/High/Medium/Low
**Services Affected**: [List]

### Timeline
- HH:MM - First alert/detection
- HH:MM - Investigation started
- HH:MM - Root cause identified
- HH:MM - Fix deployed
- HH:MM - Service restored
- HH:MM - Incident closed

### Root Cause
[Description of what caused the incident]

### Impact
- Users affected: X
- Features unavailable: [List]
- Data loss: Yes/No

### Resolution
[What was done to fix the issue]

### Prevention
- [ ] Action item 1
- [ ] Action item 2
- [ ] Action item 3

### Lessons Learned
[Key takeaways from this incident]
```

### 27.10 Emergency Contacts

| Role | Contact | Responsibility |
|------|---------|----------------|
| On-call Engineer | [PHONE] | First response |
| Tech Lead | [PHONE] | Escalation |
| DevOps | [PHONE] | Infrastructure |
| Database Admin | [PHONE] | Data recovery |
| Product Owner | [EMAIL] | Stakeholder communication |

---

  6901вҶ’

  6903вҶ’- Better TypeScript inference
  6904вҶ’- More control over generated SQL
  6905вҶ’- Faster migrations in development
  6906вҶ’- Smaller bundle size
  6907вҶ’
  6908вҶ’**4. Blueprint.js Component Library**
  6909вҶ’
  6910вҶ’The enterprise-focused component library offered:
  6911вҶ’- Dark mode out of the box
  6912вҶ’- Complex data table components
  6913вҶ’- Professional look without extensive styling
  6914вҶ’- Good documentation
  6915вҶ’
  6916вҶ’#### What Could Be Improved
  6917вҶ’
  6918вҶ’**1. State Management Complexity**
  6919вҶ’
  6920вҶ’Using @zazcart/toolkit required a learning curve. For simpler projects, React Query or SWR might suffice. The state machine approach was valuable for complex workflows but added overhead for simple CRUD operations.
  6921вҶ’
  6922вҶ’**2. Map Integration**
  6923вҶ’
  6924вҶ’Mapbox GL integration had challenges:
  6925вҶ’- Large bundle size impact
  6926вҶ’- Complex TypeScript types
  6927вҶ’- Performance tuning required for many markers
  6928вҶ’- Limited offline support
  6929вҶ’
  6930вҶ’**3. Solver Communication**
  6931вҶ’
  6932вҶ’HTTP-based solver integration had limitations:
  6933вҶ’- Timeout handling complexity
  6934вҶ’- No streaming progress updates
  6935вҶ’- Cold start latency
  6936вҶ’
  6937вҶ’Future consideration: WebSocket connection or server-sent events for real-time solver progress.
  6938вҶ’
  6939вҶ’### 26.2 Development Process
  6940вҶ’
  6941вҶ’#### Effective Practices
  6942вҶ’
  6943вҶ’**1. BMAD Methodology**
  6944вҶ’
  6945вҶ’The Business-Machine-Agent-Development workflow provided:
  6946вҶ’- Clear separation between planning and implementation
  6947вҶ’- Documented decision rationale
  6948вҶ’- Structured story creation
  6949вҶ’- Consistent development patterns
  6950вҶ’
  6951вҶ’**2. Visual Testing with Playwright**
  6952вҶ’
  6953вҶ’Mandatory visual verification caught issues that unit tests missed:
  6954вҶ’- Layout regressions
  6955вҶ’- Missing translations
  6956вҶ’- Console errors not caught by TypeScript
  6957вҶ’- Performance issues visible through timing
  6958вҶ’
  6959вҶ’**3. Incremental Commits**
  6960вҶ’
  6961вҶ’Small, focused commits enabled:
  6962вҶ’- Easy rollback of problematic changes
  6963вҶ’- Clear history for debugging
  6964вҶ’- Better code review
  6965вҶ’- Atomic feature flags
  6966вҶ’
  6967вҶ’#### Process Improvements
  6968вҶ’
  6969вҶ’**1. Earlier Integration Testing**
  6970вҶ’
  6971вҶ’Waiting too long to test API + frontend integration led to cascading fixes. Recommendation: Integration test after each API endpoint is complete.
  6972вҶ’
  6973вҶ’**2. Performance Budgets**
  6974вҶ’
  6975вҶ’Setting explicit performance targets earlier would have prevented late-stage optimization. Recommendation: Define performance budgets in the PRD phase.
  6976вҶ’
  6977вҶ’**3. Mock Data Realism**
  6978вҶ’
  6979вҶ’Early mock data was too clean. Real data revealed edge cases:
  6980вҶ’- Very long train IDs breaking layouts
  6981вҶ’- Null values in optional fields
  6982вҶ’- Date timezone issues
  6983вҶ’- Decimal precision in km calculations
  6984вҶ’
  6985вҶ’### 26.3 Technical Pitfalls
  6986вҶ’
  6987вҶ’#### React Patterns
  6988вҶ’
  6989вҶ’**Problem: Stale Closures in Animations**
  6990вҶ’
  6991вҶ’```typescript
  6992вҶ’// вқҢ WRONG - stale closure
  6993вҶ’const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
  6994вҶ’useEffect(() => {
  6995вҶ’  const interval = setInterval(() => {
  6996вҶ’    setCount(count + 1); // Always uses initial count
  6997вҶ’  }, 1000);
  6998вҶ’  return () => clearInterval(interval);
  6999вҶ’}, []);
  7000вҶ’
  7001вҶ’// вң… CORRECT - functional update
  7002вҶ’setCount(prev => prev + 1);
  7003вҶ’
  7004вҶ’// вң… BETTER - use state machine
  7005вҶ’machine.send('INCREMENT');
  7006вҶ’```
  7007вҶ’
  7008вҶ’**Problem: Excessive Re-renders**
  7009вҶ’
  7010вҶ’```typescript
  7011вҶ’// вқҢ WRONG - creates new object every render
  7012вҶ’<Map config={{ center: [lat, lng], zoom: 10 }} />
  7013вҶ’
  7014вҶ’// вң… CORRECT - memoize config
  7015вҶ’const mapConfig = useMemo(() => ({
  7016вҶ’  center: [lat, lng],
  7017вҶ’  zoom: 10
  7018вҶ’}), [lat, lng]);
  7019вҶ’<Map config={mapConfig} />
  7020вҶ’```
  7021вҶ’
  7022вҶ’#### API Design
  7023вҶ’
  7024вҶ’**Problem: Over-fetching Data**
  7025вҶ’
  7026вҶ’Initial API returned full train objects everywhere. Solution: GraphQL-style field selection or dedicated DTOs per view.
  7027вҶ’
  7028вҶ’```typescript
  7029вҶ’// вқҢ WRONG - returns everything
  7030вҶ’GET /api/trains
  7031вҶ’// Response: 45 trains Г— 20 fields Г— relations = massive payload
  7032вҶ’
  7033вҶ’// вң… CORRECT - return what's needed
  7034вҶ’GET /api/trains?fields=trainId,currentKm,fitnessStatus
  7035вҶ’// Or: dedicated endpoint
  7036вҶ’GET /api/dashboard/fleet-summary
  7037вҶ’```
  7038вҶ’
  7039вҶ’**Problem: N+1 Queries**
  7040вҶ’
  7041вҶ’```typescript
  7042вҶ’// вқҢ WRONG - N+1 queries
  7043вҶ’const trains = await db.select().from(trains);
  7044вҶ’for (const train of trains) {
  7045вҶ’  train.fleet = await db.select().from(fleets).where(eq(fleets.id, train.fleetId));
  7046вҶ’}
  7047вҶ’
  7048вҶ’// вң… CORRECT - single query with join
  7049вҶ’const trainsWithFleet = await db.query.trains.findMany({
  7050вҶ’  with: { fleet: true }
  7051вҶ’});
  7052вҶ’```
  7053вҶ’
  7054вҶ’#### Database Design
  7055вҶ’
  7056вҶ’**Problem: Missing Indexes**
  7057вҶ’
  7058вҶ’Slow queries on date-range filters. Solution: Add composite indexes for common query patterns.
  7059вҶ’
  7060вҶ’```sql
  7061вҶ’-- Before: Full table scan
  7062вҶ’SELECT * FROM allocations WHERE allocation_date BETWEEN ? AND ?;
  7063вҶ’
  7064вҶ’-- After: Index scan
  7065вҶ’CREATE INDEX allocations_date_idx ON allocations(allocation_date);
  7066вҶ’```
  7067вҶ’
  7068вҶ’**Problem: UUID Performance**
  7069вҶ’
  7070вҶ’UUIDs as primary keys had performance implications:
  7071вҶ’- Larger index size
  7072вҶ’- Random insertion pattern (non-clustered)
  7073вҶ’- Harder to debug (can't eyeball IDs)
  7074вҶ’
  7075вҶ’Mitigation: Use UUIDs for external API, but consider sequential IDs internally.
  7076вҶ’
  7077вҶ’### 26.4 Domain-Specific Learnings
  7078вҶ’
  7079вҶ’#### Railway Terminology
  7080вҶ’
  7081вҶ’| Term We Used | Correct Railway Term |
  7082вҶ’|--------------|----------------------|
  7083вҶ’| "Maintenance" | "Works" or "Overhaul" |
  7084вҶ’| "Train" | "Unit" or "Trainset" |
  7085вҶ’| "Route" | "Service" or "Diagram" |
  7086вҶ’| "Driver" | "Train Operator" or "Motorman" |
  7087вҶ’
  7088вҶ’Recommendation: Involve domain experts in terminology review.
  7089вҶ’
  7090вҶ’#### Mileage Calculation Nuances
  7091вҶ’
  7092вҶ’**Distance vs Km Run**
  7093вҶ’
  7094вҶ’- **Commercial km**: Distance with passengers
  7095вҶ’- **Dead running km**: Empty movement between locations
  7096вҶ’- **Total km**: Sum of commercial + dead running
  7097вҶ’
  7098вҶ’The solver needed to distinguish these for accurate balancing.
  7099вҶ’
  7100вҶ’**Maintenance Intervals**
  7101вҶ’
  7102вҶ’Maintenance isn't just about km:
  7103вҶ’- Calendar-based maintenance (even if low km)
  7104вҶ’- Component-specific intervals
  7105вҶ’- Regulatory inspections (time-based)
  7106вҶ’
  7107вҶ’Future enhancement: Multi-factor maintenance scheduling.
  7108вҶ’
  7109вҶ’#### Optimization Realities
  7110вҶ’
  7111вҶ’**Solver Performance vs Quality Trade-off**
  7112вҶ’
  7113вҶ’| Timeout | Solution Quality | Use Case |
  7114вҶ’|---------|------------------|----------|
  7115вҶ’| 5s | 85% optimal | Interactive preview |
  7116вҶ’| 30s | 95% optimal | Daily planning |
  7117вҶ’| 5min | 99% optimal | Weekly optimization |
  7118вҶ’
  7119вҶ’**Constraint Softness**
  7120вҶ’
  7121вҶ’Hard constraints the solver cannot break:
  7122вҶ’- Train fitness (unfit cannot run)
  7123вҶ’- Depot capacity (physical limit)
  7124вҶ’
  7125вҶ’Soft constraints that can be violated with penalty:
  7126вҶ’- Balancing preference
  7127вҶ’- Dead running minimization
  7128вҶ’- Operator preferences
  7129вҶ’
  7130вҶ’### 26.5 Team & Communication
  7131вҶ’
  7132вҶ’#### Documentation Effectiveness
  7133вҶ’
  7134вҶ’**What Helped**
  7135вҶ’
  7136вҶ’- Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) explaining "why"
  7137вҶ’- API examples in documentation
  7138вҶ’- Video walkthroughs of complex workflows
  7139вҶ’- Diagram-first communication
  7140вҶ’
  7141вҶ’**What Didn't Help**
  7142вҶ’
  7143вҶ’- Overly detailed specifications (became outdated)
  7144вҶ’- Comments that restated the code
  7145вҶ’- Documentation in separate wiki (diverged from code)
  7146вҶ’
  7147вҶ’#### Stakeholder Management
  7148вҶ’
  7149вҶ’**Effective Practices**
  7150вҶ’
  7151вҶ’- Weekly demo with working software
  7152вҶ’- Clear "definition of done" for each sprint
  7153вҶ’- Visual progress tracking (burndown chart)
  7154вҶ’- Early feedback on UI mockups
  7155вҶ’
  7156вҶ’**Challenges**
  7157вҶ’
  7158вҶ’- Requirements changes mid-sprint
  7159вҶ’- Different stakeholder priorities
  7160вҶ’- Technical debt visibility to non-technical stakeholders
  7161вҶ’
  7162вҶ’### 26.6 Recommendations for Future Projects
  7163вҶ’
  7164вҶ’1. **Start with constraints**: Define performance budgets, data volumes, and non-functional requirements early
  7165вҶ’
  7166вҶ’2. **Mock realistic data**: Generate edge cases from day one
  7167вҶ’
  7168вҶ’3. **Integrate continuously**: Don't wait for "API complete" - test integrations incrementally
  7169вҶ’
  7170вҶ’4. **Document decisions, not code**: Explain "why" in ADRs, let code explain "what"
  7171вҶ’
  7172вҶ’5. **Involve domain experts**: Railway operations knowledge is irreplaceable
  7173вҶ’
  7174вҶ’6. **Plan for demo failures**: Have backup materials ready
  7175вҶ’
  7176вҶ’7. **Performance test early**: Solver timeout issues are hard to fix late
  7177вҶ’
  7178вҶ’8. **Accessibility first**: Adding accessibility retroactively is expensive
  7179вҶ’
  7180вҶ’---
  7181вҶ’
  7182вҶ’## Chapter 27: Recovery Procedures
  7183вҶ’
  7184вҶ’### 27.1 Service Recovery Matrix
  7185вҶ’
  7186вҶ’| Service | Impact | Recovery Time | Recovery Procedure |
  7187вҶ’|---------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
  7188вҶ’| Frontend | UI unavailable | 5 min | Vercel rollback |
  7189вҶ’| API | No data access | 10 min | Railway rollback + DB check |
  7190вҶ’| Solver | No recommendations | 15 min | Railway rollback + cache clear |
  7191вҶ’| Database | Complete outage | 30 min | Point-in-time restore |
  7192вҶ’| Redis | Degraded performance | 5 min | Redis restart or failover |
  7193вҶ’
  7194вҶ’### 27.2 Frontend Recovery
  7195вҶ’
  7196вҶ’#### Symptom: Blank Screen
  7197вҶ’
  7198вҶ’**Diagnosis Steps**
  7199вҶ’
  7200вҶ’1. Check browser console for errors
  7201вҶ’2. Verify API endpoint connectivity
  7202вҶ’3. Check Vercel deployment status
  7203вҶ’4. Review recent deployments
  7204вҶ’
  7205вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7206вҶ’
  7207вҶ’```bash
  7208вҶ’# 1. Check deployment status
  7209вҶ’vercel ls --app mms-web
  7210вҶ’
  7211вҶ’# 2. Rollback to last working deployment
  7212вҶ’vercel rollback <last-good-deployment-id>
  7213вҶ’
  7214вҶ’# 3. If rollback fails, redeploy from known good commit
  7215вҶ’git checkout <good-commit>
  7216вҶ’vercel --prod
  7217вҶ’```
  7218вҶ’
  7219вҶ’#### Symptom: API Connection Errors
  7220вҶ’
  7221вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7222вҶ’
  7223вҶ’```javascript
  7224вҶ’// Check in browser console
  7225вҶ’fetch('/api/health').then(r => r.json()).then(console.log)
  7226вҶ’```
  7227вҶ’
  7228вҶ’**Recovery**
  7229вҶ’
  7230вҶ’1. Verify `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` environment variable
  7231вҶ’2. Check API service health
  7232вҶ’3. Verify CORS configuration
  7233вҶ’4. Check for SSL certificate issues
  7234вҶ’
  7235вҶ’### 27.3 API Recovery
  7236вҶ’
  7237вҶ’#### Symptom: 500 Errors
  7238вҶ’
  7239вҶ’**Diagnosis Steps**
  7240вҶ’
  7241вҶ’```bash
  7242вҶ’# 1. Check logs
  7243вҶ’railway logs --app mms-api
  7244вҶ’
  7245вҶ’# 2. Check database connection
  7246вҶ’curl https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/health
  7247вҶ’
  7248вҶ’# 3. Verify environment variables
  7249вҶ’railway variables --app mms-api
  7250вҶ’```
  7251вҶ’
  7252вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7253вҶ’
  7254вҶ’```bash
  7255вҶ’# Option 1: Restart service
  7256вҶ’railway restart --app mms-api
  7257вҶ’
  7258вҶ’# Option 2: Rollback deployment
  7259вҶ’railway rollback --app mms-api
  7260вҶ’
  7261вҶ’# Option 3: Database connection fix
  7262вҶ’# Check DATABASE_URL is valid
  7263вҶ’# Test connection manually
  7264вҶ’psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"
  7265вҶ’```
  7266вҶ’
  7267вҶ’#### Symptom: Slow Responses
  7268вҶ’
  7269вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7270вҶ’
  7271вҶ’```bash
  7272вҶ’# Check response times
  7273вҶ’curl -w "@curl-format.txt" -o /dev/null -s https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/trains
  7274вҶ’
  7275вҶ’# curl-format.txt:
  7276вҶ’#   time_namelookup:  %{time_namelookup}s
  7277вҶ’#   time_connect:     %{time_connect}s
  7278вҶ’#   time_appconnect:  %{time_appconnect}s
  7279вҶ’#   time_total:       %{time_total}s
  7280вҶ’```
  7281вҶ’
  7282вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7283вҶ’
  7284вҶ’1. Scale up Railway instance
  7285вҶ’2. Check database query performance
  7286вҶ’3. Clear Redis cache
  7287вҶ’4. Review recent code changes for N+1 queries
  7288вҶ’
  7289вҶ’### 27.4 Solver Recovery
  7290вҶ’
  7291вҶ’#### Symptom: Solver Timeout
  7292вҶ’
  7293вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7294вҶ’
  7295вҶ’```bash
  7296вҶ’# Check solver logs
  7297вҶ’railway logs --app mms-solver
  7298вҶ’
  7299вҶ’# Test solver endpoint
  7300вҶ’curl -X POST https://solver.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/optimize/allocation \
  7301вҶ’  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  7302вҶ’  -d '{"trains": [], "services": []}'
  7303вҶ’```
  7304вҶ’
  7305вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7306вҶ’
  7307вҶ’```bash
  7308вҶ’# 1. Increase timeout limit
  7309вҶ’railway variables set MAX_SOLVER_TIME=60 --app mms-solver
  7310вҶ’
  7311вҶ’# 2. Scale up compute
  7312вҶ’railway scale --app mms-solver --memory 2GB
  7313вҶ’
  7314вҶ’# 3. Reduce problem size (temporary)
  7315вҶ’# Implement pagination or batch processing
  7316вҶ’```
  7317вҶ’
  7318вҶ’#### Symptom: Solver Returns Infeasible
  7319вҶ’
  7320вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7321вҶ’
  7322вҶ’Check solver response for constraint violations:
  7323вҶ’- All trains unfit?
  7324вҶ’- Services exceed fleet capacity?
  7325вҶ’- Depot capacity too low?
  7326вҶ’
  7327вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7328вҶ’
  7329вҶ’1. Review input data for invalid values
  7330вҶ’2. Check for data corruption
  7331вҶ’3. Verify constraint parameters
  7332вҶ’4. Implement constraint relaxation fallback
  7333вҶ’
  7334вҶ’### 27.5 Database Recovery
  7335вҶ’
  7336вҶ’#### Symptom: Connection Failures
  7337вҶ’
  7338вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7339вҶ’
  7340вҶ’```bash
  7341вҶ’# Test connection
  7342вҶ’psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"
  7343вҶ’
  7344вҶ’# Check connection pool
  7345вҶ’psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity"
  7346вҶ’```
  7347вҶ’
  7348вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7349вҶ’
  7350вҶ’```bash
  7351вҶ’# 1. Restart connection pool
  7352вҶ’railway restart --app mms-api
  7353вҶ’
  7354вҶ’# 2. Increase pool size (if exhausted)
  7355вҶ’# Update DATABASE_URL with ?pool_size=20
  7356вҶ’
  7357вҶ’# 3. Kill idle connections
  7358вҶ’psql $DATABASE_URL -c "
  7359вҶ’  SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
  7360вҶ’  FROM pg_stat_activity
  7361вҶ’  WHERE state = 'idle'
  7362вҶ’  AND query_start < now() - interval '5 minutes';
  7363вҶ’"
  7364вҶ’```
  7365вҶ’
  7366вҶ’#### Symptom: Data Corruption
  7367вҶ’
  7368вҶ’**Diagnosis**
  7369вҶ’
  7370вҶ’```sql
  7371вҶ’-- Check for orphaned records
  7372вҶ’SELECT t.id FROM trains t
  7373вҶ’LEFT JOIN fleets f ON t.fleet_id = f.id
  7374вҶ’WHERE f.id IS NULL AND t.fleet_id IS NOT NULL;
  7375вҶ’
  7376вҶ’-- Check for invalid values
  7377вҶ’SELECT * FROM trains WHERE current_km < 0 OR current_km > 10000000;
  7378вҶ’```
  7379вҶ’
  7380вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7381вҶ’
  7382вҶ’```bash
  7383вҶ’# 1. Identify corruption scope
  7384вҶ’# Run integrity checks
  7385вҶ’
  7386вҶ’# 2. Point-in-time recovery (if needed)
  7387вҶ’# Contact Neon support for PITR
  7388вҶ’
  7389вҶ’# 3. Restore from backup
  7390вҶ’pg_restore -d $DATABASE_URL backup.dump
  7391вҶ’```
  7392вҶ’
  7393вҶ’#### Backup Procedures
  7394вҶ’
  7395вҶ’**Automated Backups**
  7396вҶ’
  7397вҶ’Neon provides automatic point-in-time recovery. For additional safety:
  7398вҶ’
  7399вҶ’```bash
  7400вҶ’# Daily backup script
  7401вҶ’pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql
  7402вҶ’
  7403вҶ’# Upload to cloud storage
  7404вҶ’aws s3 cp backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql s3://mms-backups/
  7405вҶ’```
  7406вҶ’
  7407вҶ’**Restore from Backup**
  7408вҶ’
  7409вҶ’```bash
  7410вҶ’# 1. Create new database
  7411вҶ’neon databases create mms_restored
  7412вҶ’
  7413вҶ’# 2. Restore data
  7414вҶ’psql $NEW_DATABASE_URL < backups/mms-20250115.sql
  7415вҶ’
  7416вҶ’# 3. Update API to use new database
  7417вҶ’railway variables set DATABASE_URL=$NEW_DATABASE_URL --app mms-api
  7418вҶ’```
  7419вҶ’
  7420вҶ’### 27.6 Redis Recovery
  7421вҶ’
  7422вҶ’#### Symptom: Cache Miss Storm
  7423вҶ’
  7424вҶ’When Redis fails, all requests hit the database:
  7425вҶ’
  7426вҶ’**Recovery Actions**
  7427вҶ’
  7428вҶ’```bash
  7429вҶ’# 1. Restart Redis
  7430вҶ’railway restart --app mms-redis
  7431вҶ’
  7432вҶ’# 2. Implement graceful degradation
  7433вҶ’# API should work without cache (slower)
  7434вҶ’
  7435вҶ’# 3. Warm up cache
  7436вҶ’curl -X POST https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/cache/warm
  7437вҶ’```
  7438вҶ’
  7439вҶ’#### Symptom: Memory Exhaustion
  7440вҶ’
  7441вҶ’```bash
  7442вҶ’# Check memory usage
  7443вҶ’redis-cli INFO memory
  7444вҶ’
  7445вҶ’# Clear specific patterns
  7446вҶ’redis-cli KEYS "cache:train:*" | xargs redis-cli DEL
  7447вҶ’
  7448вҶ’# Set memory policy
  7449вҶ’redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
  7450вҶ’```
  7451вҶ’
  7452вҶ’### 27.7 Data Recovery Procedures
  7453вҶ’
  7454вҶ’#### Restore Deleted Trains

Post-demo review:
```bash
# Export demo session metrics
bun run demo:export-metrics

# Generate demo report
```


---

# Part VI: Lessons Learned & Recovery

This part documents lessons learned during the development process, common pitfalls to avoid, and recovery procedures for various failure scenarios.

---

## Chapter 26: Lessons Learned

### 26.1 Architecture Decisions

#### What Worked Well

**1. Monorepo Structure**

Keeping frontend, API, and solver in a single repository provided:
- Atomic commits across system boundaries
- Shared TypeScript types
- Simplified CI/CD pipelines
- Easy local development

**2. Dedicated Python Solver**

Separating the optimization engine brought:
- Access to OR-Tools' mature CP-SAT solver
- Independent scaling of compute-intensive operations
- Clear API contract between services
- Ability to test solver logic in isolation

**3. Drizzle ORM over Prisma**

Drizzle provided advantages for this project:
- Better TypeScript inference
- More control over generated SQL
- Faster migrations in development
- Smaller bundle size

**4. Blueprint.js Component Library**

The enterprise-focused component library offered:
- Dark mode out of the box
- Complex data table components
- Professional look without extensive styling
- Good documentation

#### What Could Be Improved

**1. State Management Complexity**

Using @zazcart/toolkit required a learning curve. For simpler projects, React Query or SWR might suffice. The state machine approach was valuable for complex workflows but added overhead for simple CRUD operations.

**2. Map Integration**

Mapbox GL integration had challenges:
- Large bundle size impact
- Complex TypeScript types
- Performance tuning required for many markers
- Limited offline support

**3. Solver Communication**

HTTP-based solver integration had limitations:
- Timeout handling complexity
- No streaming progress updates
- Cold start latency

Future consideration: WebSocket connection or server-sent events for real-time solver progress.

### 26.2 Development Process

#### Effective Practices

**1. BMAD Methodology**

The Business-Machine-Agent-Development workflow provided:
- Clear separation between planning and implementation
- Documented decision rationale
- Structured story creation
- Consistent development patterns

**2. Visual Testing with Playwright**

Mandatory visual verification caught issues that unit tests missed:
- Layout regressions
- Missing translations
- Console errors not caught by TypeScript
- Performance issues visible through timing

**3. Incremental Commits**

Small, focused commits enabled:
- Easy rollback of problematic changes
- Clear history for debugging
- Better code review
- Atomic feature flags

#### Process Improvements

**1. Earlier Integration Testing**

Waiting too long to test API + frontend integration led to cascading fixes. Recommendation: Integration test after each API endpoint is complete.

**2. Performance Budgets**

Setting explicit performance targets earlier would have prevented late-stage optimization. Recommendation: Define performance budgets in the PRD phase.

**3. Mock Data Realism**

Early mock data was too clean. Real data revealed edge cases:
- Very long train IDs breaking layouts
- Null values in optional fields
- Date timezone issues
- Decimal precision in km calculations

### 26.3 Technical Pitfalls

#### React Patterns

**Problem: Stale Closures in Animations**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - stale closure
const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
useEffect(() => {
  const interval = setInterval(() => {
    setCount(count + 1); // Always uses initial count
  }, 1000);
  return () => clearInterval(interval);
}, []);

// вң… CORRECT - functional update
setCount(prev => prev + 1);

// вң… BETTER - use state machine
machine.send('INCREMENT');
```

**Problem: Excessive Re-renders**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - creates new object every render
<Map config={{ center: [lat, lng], zoom: 10 }} />

// вң… CORRECT - memoize config
const mapConfig = useMemo(() => ({
  center: [lat, lng],
  zoom: 10
}), [lat, lng]);
<Map config={mapConfig} />
```

#### API Design

**Problem: Over-fetching Data**

Initial API returned full train objects everywhere. Solution: GraphQL-style field selection or dedicated DTOs per view.

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - returns everything
GET /api/trains
// Response: 45 trains Г— 20 fields Г— relations = massive payload

// вң… CORRECT - return what's needed
GET /api/trains?fields=trainId,currentKm,fitnessStatus
// Or: dedicated endpoint
GET /api/dashboard/fleet-summary
```

**Problem: N+1 Queries**

```typescript
// вқҢ WRONG - N+1 queries
const trains = await db.select().from(trains);
for (const train of trains) {
  train.fleet = await db.select().from(fleets).where(eq(fleets.id, train.fleetId));
}

// вң… CORRECT - single query with join
const trainsWithFleet = await db.query.trains.findMany({
  with: { fleet: true }
});
```

#### Database Design

**Problem: Missing Indexes**

Slow queries on date-range filters. Solution: Add composite indexes for common query patterns.

```sql
-- Before: Full table scan
SELECT * FROM allocations WHERE allocation_date BETWEEN ? AND ?;

-- After: Index scan
CREATE INDEX allocations_date_idx ON allocations(allocation_date);
```

**Problem: UUID Performance**

UUIDs as primary keys had performance implications:
- Larger index size
- Random insertion pattern (non-clustered)
- Harder to debug (can't eyeball IDs)

Mitigation: Use UUIDs for external API, but consider sequential IDs internally.

### 26.4 Domain-Specific Learnings

#### Railway Terminology

| Term We Used | Correct Railway Term |
|--------------|----------------------|
| "Maintenance" | "Works" or "Overhaul" |
| "Train" | "Unit" or "Trainset" |
| "Route" | "Service" or "Diagram" |
| "Driver" | "Train Operator" or "Motorman" |

Recommendation: Involve domain experts in terminology review.

#### Mileage Calculation Nuances

**Distance vs Km Run**

- **Commercial km**: Distance with passengers
- **Dead running km**: Empty movement between locations
- **Total km**: Sum of commercial + dead running

The solver needed to distinguish these for accurate balancing.

**Maintenance Intervals**

Maintenance isn't just about km:
- Calendar-based maintenance (even if low km)
- Component-specific intervals
- Regulatory inspections (time-based)

Future enhancement: Multi-factor maintenance scheduling.

#### Optimization Realities

**Solver Performance vs Quality Trade-off**

| Timeout | Solution Quality | Use Case |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| 5s | 85% optimal | Interactive preview |
| 30s | 95% optimal | Daily planning |
| 5min | 99% optimal | Weekly optimization |

**Constraint Softness**

Hard constraints the solver cannot break:
- Train fitness (unfit cannot run)
- Depot capacity (physical limit)

Soft constraints that can be violated with penalty:
- Balancing preference
- Dead running minimization
- Operator preferences

### 26.5 Team & Communication

#### Documentation Effectiveness

**What Helped**

- Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) explaining "why"
- API examples in documentation
- Video walkthroughs of complex workflows
- Diagram-first communication

**What Didn't Help**

- Overly detailed specifications (became outdated)
- Comments that restated the code
- Documentation in separate wiki (diverged from code)

#### Stakeholder Management

**Effective Practices**

- Weekly demo with working software
- Clear "definition of done" for each sprint
- Visual progress tracking (burndown chart)
- Early feedback on UI mockups

**Challenges**

- Requirements changes mid-sprint
- Different stakeholder priorities
- Technical debt visibility to non-technical stakeholders

### 26.6 Recommendations for Future Projects

1. **Start with constraints**: Define performance budgets, data volumes, and non-functional requirements early

2. **Mock realistic data**: Generate edge cases from day one

3. **Integrate continuously**: Don't wait for "API complete" - test integrations incrementally

4. **Document decisions, not code**: Explain "why" in ADRs, let code explain "what"

5. **Involve domain experts**: Railway operations knowledge is irreplaceable

6. **Plan for demo failures**: Have backup materials ready

7. **Performance test early**: Solver timeout issues are hard to fix late

8. **Accessibility first**: Adding accessibility retroactively is expensive

---

## Chapter 27: Recovery Procedures

### 27.1 Service Recovery Matrix

| Service | Impact | Recovery Time | Recovery Procedure |
|---------|--------|---------------|-------------------|
| Frontend | UI unavailable | 5 min | Vercel rollback |
| API | No data access | 10 min | Railway rollback + DB check |
| Solver | No recommendations | 15 min | Railway rollback + cache clear |
| Database | Complete outage | 30 min | Point-in-time restore |
| Redis | Degraded performance | 5 min | Redis restart or failover |

### 27.2 Frontend Recovery

#### Symptom: Blank Screen

**Diagnosis Steps**

1. Check browser console for errors
2. Verify API endpoint connectivity
3. Check Vercel deployment status
4. Review recent deployments

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Check deployment status
vercel ls --app mms-web

# 2. Rollback to last working deployment
vercel rollback <last-good-deployment-id>

# 3. If rollback fails, redeploy from known good commit
git checkout <good-commit>
vercel --prod
```

#### Symptom: API Connection Errors

**Diagnosis**

```javascript
// Check in browser console
fetch('/api/health').then(r => r.json()).then(console.log)
```

**Recovery**

1. Verify `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL` environment variable
2. Check API service health
3. Verify CORS configuration
4. Check for SSL certificate issues

### 27.3 API Recovery

#### Symptom: 500 Errors

**Diagnosis Steps**

```bash
# 1. Check logs
railway logs --app mms-api

# 2. Check database connection
curl https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/health

# 3. Verify environment variables
railway variables --app mms-api
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# Option 1: Restart service
railway restart --app mms-api

# Option 2: Rollback deployment
railway rollback --app mms-api

# Option 3: Database connection fix
# Check DATABASE_URL is valid
# Test connection manually
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"
```

#### Symptom: Slow Responses

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Check response times
curl -w "@curl-format.txt" -o /dev/null -s https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/trains

# curl-format.txt:
#   time_namelookup:  %{time_namelookup}s
#   time_connect:     %{time_connect}s
#   time_appconnect:  %{time_appconnect}s
#   time_total:       %{time_total}s
```

**Recovery Actions**

1. Scale up Railway instance
2. Check database query performance
3. Clear Redis cache
4. Review recent code changes for N+1 queries

### 27.4 Solver Recovery

#### Symptom: Solver Timeout

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Check solver logs
railway logs --app mms-solver

# Test solver endpoint
curl -X POST https://solver.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/optimize/allocation \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"trains": [], "services": []}'
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Increase timeout limit
railway variables set MAX_SOLVER_TIME=60 --app mms-solver

# 2. Scale up compute
railway scale --app mms-solver --memory 2GB

# 3. Reduce problem size (temporary)
# Implement pagination or batch processing
```

#### Symptom: Solver Returns Infeasible

**Diagnosis**

Check solver response for constraint violations:
- All trains unfit?
- Services exceed fleet capacity?
- Depot capacity too low?

**Recovery Actions**

1. Review input data for invalid values
2. Check for data corruption
3. Verify constraint parameters
4. Implement constraint relaxation fallback

### 27.5 Database Recovery

#### Symptom: Connection Failures

**Diagnosis**

```bash
# Test connection
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT 1"

# Check connection pool
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity"
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Restart connection pool
railway restart --app mms-api

# 2. Increase pool size (if exhausted)
# Update DATABASE_URL with ?pool_size=20

# 3. Kill idle connections
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "
  SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid)
  FROM pg_stat_activity
  WHERE state = 'idle'
  AND query_start < now() - interval '5 minutes';
"
```

#### Symptom: Data Corruption

**Diagnosis**

```sql
-- Check for orphaned records
SELECT t.id FROM trains t
LEFT JOIN fleets f ON t.fleet_id = f.id
WHERE f.id IS NULL AND t.fleet_id IS NOT NULL;

-- Check for invalid values
SELECT * FROM trains WHERE current_km < 0 OR current_km > 10000000;
```

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Identify corruption scope
# Run integrity checks

# 2. Point-in-time recovery (if needed)
# Contact Neon support for PITR

# 3. Restore from backup
pg_restore -d $DATABASE_URL backup.dump
```

#### Backup Procedures

**Automated Backups**

Neon provides automatic point-in-time recovery. For additional safety:

```bash
# Daily backup script
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql

# Upload to cloud storage
aws s3 cp backups/mms-$(date +%Y%m%d).sql s3://mms-backups/
```

**Restore from Backup**

```bash
# 1. Create new database
neon databases create mms_restored

# 2. Restore data
psql $NEW_DATABASE_URL < backups/mms-20250115.sql

# 3. Update API to use new database
railway variables set DATABASE_URL=$NEW_DATABASE_URL --app mms-api
```

### 27.6 Redis Recovery

#### Symptom: Cache Miss Storm

When Redis fails, all requests hit the database:

**Recovery Actions**

```bash
# 1. Restart Redis
railway restart --app mms-redis

# 2. Implement graceful degradation
# API should work without cache (slower)

# 3. Warm up cache
curl -X POST https://api.mms-mfc.railway.app/api/cache/warm
```

#### Symptom: Memory Exhaustion

```bash
# Check memory usage
redis-cli INFO memory

# Clear specific patterns
redis-cli KEYS "cache:train:*" | xargs redis-cli DEL

# Set memory policy
redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
```

### 27.7 Data Recovery Procedures

#### Restore Deleted Trains

```sql
-- If soft delete is implemented
UPDATE trains SET deleted_at = NULL WHERE id = '<train-id>';

-- If hard delete, restore from audit log
INSERT INTO trains (id, train_id, fleet_id, current_km, ...)
SELECT id, train_id, fleet_id, current_km, ...
FROM audit_log
WHERE table_name = 'trains'
  AND record_id = '<train-id>'
  AND operation = 'DELETE'
ORDER BY timestamp DESC
LIMIT 1;
```

#### Restore Deleted Allocations

```sql
-- Restore allocations for a specific date
INSERT INTO allocations (service_id, train_id, allocation_date, status)
SELECT service_id, train_id, allocation_date, 'restored'
FROM allocations_backup
WHERE allocation_date = '2025-01-15';
```

#### Fix Incorrect Mileage

```sql
-- Identify incorrect values
SELECT * FROM mileage_history
WHERE km_value > 1000000 OR km_value < 0;

-- Correct using previous valid record
UPDATE mileage_history mh
SET km_value = (
  SELECT km_value
  FROM mileage_history mh2
  WHERE mh2.train_id = mh.train_id
    AND mh2.recorded_date < mh.recorded_date
    AND mh2.km_value BETWEEN 0 AND 1000000
  ORDER BY recorded_date DESC
  LIMIT 1
)
WHERE km_value > 1000000 OR km_value < 0;
```

### 27.8 Disaster Recovery

#### Complete System Failure

**Recovery Steps**

1. **Assess scope** (5 min)
   - Which services are down?
   - Is data intact?
   - What was last known good state?

2. **Communicate** (immediately)
   - Notify stakeholders
   - Update status page
   - Set expectations for recovery time

3. **Database recovery** (30 min)
   - Restore from most recent backup
   - Verify data integrity
   - Update connection strings

4. **Service recovery** (in order)
   - Redis (5 min)
   - Solver (10 min)
   - API (10 min)
   - Frontend (5 min)

5. **Verification** (15 min)
   - Run smoke tests
   - Check critical paths
   - Verify data consistency

6. **Post-incident** (after recovery)
   - Document timeline
   - Identify root cause
   - Implement preventive measures

#### Regional Failover

If primary region (GRU1) is unavailable:

```bash
# 1. Deploy to backup region
vercel deploy --prod --region fra1

# 2. Update DNS
# Change A record to point to backup region

# 3. Update API URL
vercel env add NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL https://api-backup.mms-mfc.railway.app production
```

### 27.9 Incident Response Template

```markdown
## Incident Report: [TITLE]

**Date/Time**: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM UTC
**Duration**: X hours Y minutes
**Severity**: Critical/High/Medium/Low
**Services Affected**: [List]

### Timeline
- HH:MM - First alert/detection
- HH:MM - Investigation started
- HH:MM - Root cause identified
- HH:MM - Fix deployed
- HH:MM - Service restored
- HH:MM - Incident closed

### Root Cause
[Description of what caused the incident]

### Impact
- Users affected: X
- Features unavailable: [List]
- Data loss: Yes/No

### Resolution
[What was done to fix the issue]

### Prevention
- [ ] Action item 1
- [ ] Action item 2
- [ ] Action item 3

### Lessons Learned
[Key takeaways from this incident]
```

### 27.10 Emergency Contacts

| Role | Contact | Responsibility |
|------|---------|----------------|
| On-call Engineer | [PHONE] | First response |
| Tech Lead | [PHONE] | Escalation |
| DevOps | [PHONE] | Infrastructure |
| Database Admin | [PHONE] | Data recovery |
| Product Owner | [EMAIL] | Stakeholder communication |

---

# MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
## Complete Product & Technical Specification

**Version:** 2.0.0
**Date:** 2026-01-05
**Status:** Definitive Specification for Rebuild
**Audience:** UX/Product Team, Development Team, Stakeholders

---

## Document Purpose

This document serves as the **Single Source of Truth** for rebuilding the MMS (Metro Mileage Management System) from scratch. It consolidates all requirements, architecture decisions, UX specifications, and lessons learned from the initial Claude Code implementation.

The original implementation encountered complexity issues that resulted in incomplete features and inconsistent behavior. This specification provides a complete, unambiguous reference for human teams to deliver a production-ready demo.

---

## Table of Contents

### Part I: Business Context & Strategy

#### 1. Executive Summary
- 1.1 Project Overview
- 1.2 Business Objective
- 1.3 Success Metrics
- 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

> **Covers:** High-level project goals, the competitive bid context, what success looks like, and the 12-week timeline with key milestones.

#### 2. Railway Domain Fundamentals
- 2.1 Essential Glossary
- 2.2 Business Entities & Relationships
- 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy
- 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)
- 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

> **Covers:** Railway-specific terminology (EN 15380), entity relationships, maintenance intervals (PM1/PM2/HM), and domain constraints that are critical for credibility.

#### 3. Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning
- 3.1 Competitor Landscape
- 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis
- 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities
- 3.4 MMS Differentiators

> **Covers:** Analysis of DELMIA Quintiq, Tracsis, Railnova, SignatureRail; identified gaps in the market; and how MMS positions itself.

#### 4. Demo Strategy & Presentation
- 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth
- 4.2 Wow Moments

# PART I: BUSINESS CONTEXT & STRATEGY

---

## Chapter 1: Executive Summary

### 1.1 Project Overview

**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.

**Core Problem Statement:**

Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:

1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints

**Solution Vision:**

MMS addresses all three pain points through:
- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
- What-if simulation for strategic planning

### 1.2 Business Objective

**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.

**Target Audience:**
- Operations Director (decision maker)
- Client's CTO (technical validator)
- Maintenance leadership (domain experts)

**Success Statement:**

> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**

**Key Differentiators from Competitors:**

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
| Unified Maintenance вҶ” Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |

### 1.3 Success Metrics

**User Success Metrics:**

| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |

**Business Success Metrics:**
